made.heading: Order in Council
made.made.at: At Wellington this 15th day of October 2018
made.made.present: Present:Her Excellency the Governor-General in Council

para.text: The Parliamentary Counsel Office has made editorial and format changes to this version using the powers under subpart 2 of Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019.
Note 4 at the end of this version provides a list of the amendments included in it.

cover.reprint-note.admin-office: These regulations are administered by the Ministry of Social Development.

para.text: These regulations are made under sections 418 to 451 of the Social Security Act 2018—
on the advice and with the consent of the Executive Council; and

for exemptions from obligations regulations made under section 431(1)﻿(e) of the Act (see subparts 8 and 9 of Part 3 of these regulations), on the Minister’s recommendation made under section 431(4) and (5) of the Act; and
for pre-benefit activities regulations made under section 432(1) of the Act (see subpart 1 of Part 4 of these regulations), on the Minister’s recommendation made under section 432(2) of the Act; and

for benefit stand-down exemption regulations made under section 440(1) and (2)﻿(a) of the Act (see subpart 2 of Part 6 of these regulations), on the Minister’s recommendation made under section 440(4) and (5) of the Act; and

for suspension of debt recovery regulations made under section 448(1) of the Act (see subpart 11 of Part 6 of these regulations), on the Minister’s recommendation made under section 448(3) of the Act.

history.history-note: Enacting statement paragraph (e): editorial change made by the PCO, on 5 August 2024, under sections 86(1) and 87(l)﻿(iii) of the Legislation Act 2019 (2019 No 58).

prov.heading: Title

para.text: These regulations are the Social Security Regulations 2018.

prov.heading: Commencement

para.text: These regulations (other than regulations 77, 78, and 147(d)) come into force on 26 November 2018.
Regulations 77, 78, and 147(d) come into force on the commencement of sections 165 and 168 of the Act.

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In these regulations, unless the context otherwise requires,—
Act means the Social Security Act 2018

adopted reciprocity agreement means a reciprocity agreement that is adopted under section 380 of the Act, or the provisions of which are given force and effect so far as they relate to New Zealand by an order made under section 380 of the Act
contracted service provider has the meaning given to it by section 373 of the Act
contracted youth service means a service provided to a young person by a contracted service provider under a section 373 services contract

dentist means a health practitioner who is, or is deemed to be, registered with the Dental Council established by section 114(2) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 as a practitioner of the profession of dentistry
emergency housing contribution means an amount that—
is a contribution to the cost of emergency housing assistance paid to or for the credit of a person under the Emergency Housing Grants Programme; and

is under the terms and conditions of that programme payable as an expense by, or recoverable as a debt of the kind referred to in regulation 206(d) from, the person to or for the credit of whom that assistance is paid
Emergency Housing Grants Programme means the Emergency Housing Grants Programme approved and established under section 101 of the Act
Employment and Work Readiness Assistance Programme means the Employment and Work Readiness Assistance Programme—
approved and established under section 124(1)﻿(d) of the Social Security Act 1964 on 6 March 2014; and
saved by clause 21 of Schedule 1 of the Social Security Act 2018 as if it were approved and established under section 101 of that Act
family violence includes, but is not limited to, family violence as that term is defined in section 9 of the Family Violence Act 2018
foster child means a child (except a dependent child) placed in a person’s charge under section 362 of the Oranga Tamariki Act 1989

midwife means a health practitioner who is, or is deemed to be, registered with the Midwifery Council established by section 114(3) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 as a practitioner of the profession of midwifery

nurse practitioner means a health practitioner who is, or is deemed to be, registered with the Nursing Council of New Zealand continued by section 114(1)﻿(a) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 as a practitioner of the profession of nursing and whose scope of practice permits the performance of nurse practitioner functions

psychologist means a health practitioner who is, or is deemed to be, registered with the Psychologists Board continued by section 114(1)﻿(a) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 as a practitioner of the profession of psychology
public transport service means a service for the carriage of passengers for hire or reward by means of—
a large passenger service vehicle (as defined in section 2(1) of the Land Transport Act 1998):
a small passenger service vehicle (as defined in section 2(1) of the Land Transport Act 1998):
a ferry:
a hovercraft:
a rail vehicle (as defined in section 4(1) of the Railways Act 2005):
any other mode of transport (other than air transport) that is available to the public generally

reciprocity agreement means an agreement (for example, one entitled a Convention), or an alteration to an agreement, with the Government of another country providing for reciprocity in respect of matters relating to social security monetary benefits
Special Needs Grants Programme means the Special Needs Grants Programme—
approved and established under section 124(1)﻿(d) of the Social Security Act 1964; and
saved by clause 21 of Schedule 1 of the Social Security Act 2018 as if it were approved and established under section 101 of that Act; and
as in force (as amended under section 101 of that Act with effect) on or after 30 March 2020
Training Incentive Allowance Programme means the Training Incentive Allowance Programme approved and established under section 101 of the Act on 24 May 2021
transitional housing contribution means an amount that—
is a contribution to the cost of transitional housing funded partly or wholly by assistance paid by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development; and
is under the terms and conditions of that assistance payable as an expense by, or recoverable as a debt from, a person provided that housing because of that assistance.

In these regulations, unless the context otherwise requires, terms used and not defined, but listed in Schedule 2 of the Act, have the meanings given to them (for the relevant provisions or purposes of the Act) by the definitions set out, or referred to, in Schedule 2 of the Act.

history.history-note: Regulation 3(1) emergency housing contribution: inserted, on 30 March 2020, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Emergency Housing and Transitional Housing) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/20).
Regulation 3(1) emergency housing contribution paragraph (a): amended, on 26 August 2024, by regulation 4(1) of the Social Security (Emergency Housing) Amendment Regulations 2024 (SL 2024/137).
Regulation 3(1) Emergency Housing Grants Programme: inserted, on 26 August 2024, by regulation 4(2) of the Social Security (Emergency Housing) Amendment Regulations 2024 (SL 2024/137).

Regulation 3(1) Employment and Work Readiness Assistance Programme: inserted, on 17 November 2025, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Youth Money Management) Amendment Regulations 2025 (SL 2025/223).
Regulation 3(1) family violence: amended, on 1 July 2019, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Family Violence) Amendment Regulations 2019 (LI 2019/99).
Regulation 3(1) public transport service: inserted, on 10 October 2022, by regulation 4 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2022 (SL 2022/251).

Regulation 3(1) Special Needs Grants Programme: inserted, on 30 March 2020, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Emergency Housing and Transitional Housing) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/20).
Regulation 3(1) Training Incentive Allowance Programme: inserted, on 17 November 2025, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Youth Money Management) Amendment Regulations 2025 (SL 2025/223).

Regulation 3(1) transitional housing contribution: inserted, on 30 March 2020, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Emergency Housing and Transitional Housing) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/20).

part.heading: Preliminary provision

prov.heading: Transitional, savings, and related provisions

para.text: The transitional, savings, and related provisions (if any) set out in Schedule 1 have effect according to their terms.

part.heading: Assistance

prov.heading: Guide to Part 2

para.text: Part 2 deals with various topics as follows:

subpart 1 (regulations 6, 7, and 7A) deals with a beneficiary’s absence from or presence in New Zealand, the treatment of persons who have had income tax deducted or withheld from earnings from employment overseas, and circumstances in which certain holders of, or people who would have been eligible for, a Christchurch response visa are taken to meet the residential requirement:
subpart 2 (regulations 8 and 9) deals with prescribing health practitioners for jobseeker support certificates and medical examinations:
subpart 3 (regulations 10 and 11) prescribes a minimum period of eligibility for, and health practitioners for certificates and medical examinations for, supported living payments:
subpart 4 (regulations 12 to 14) sets out criteria for youth payment incentive payments:
subpart 5 (regulations 15 to 19) provides for matters related to accommodation supplements:
subpart 6 (regulations 20 to 48) provides for childcare assistance:
subpart 7 (regulation 49) deals with prescribing health practitioners for certificates and medical examinations for child disability allowances:
subpart 8 (regulations 50 to 52) provides for special categories of eligibility for, and prescribes health practitioners for medical examinations for, disability allowances:
subpart 9 (regulations 53 to 55) provides for methods and amounts of payments of funeral grants:
subpart 10 (regulations 56 to 71) provides for temporary additional support.

history.history-note: Regulation 5(a): replaced, on 3 June 2019, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Residential Requirement—Christchurch Response Visa) Amendment Regulations 2019 (LI 2019/115).

subpart.heading: Beneficiary’s absence from or presence in New Zealand and persons taxed under New Zealand law

prov.heading: Effect of absence of beneficiary from New Zealand on residential requirement

para.text: This regulation applies to a person (P) who—
leaves New Zealand while in receipt of a benefit; and
returns to take up ordinary residence in New Zealand within 5 years; and
later applies for a benefit of the same class.
P is not required to comply with the residential requirement applicable in the case of an original application for a benefit of that class.

prov.heading: Persons who have had income tax deducted or withheld on earnings from employment overseas to be treated as resident and present in New Zealand

para.text: This regulation applies to a person (P) who was employed outside New Zealand if, with respect to P’s period of employment, P’s employer (or any other relevant person) makes—

PAYE income payments (as that term is used in the Income Tax Act 2007) from which P, P’s employer, or any other relevant person must withhold an amount of tax under the PAYE rules as defined in that Act:
source deduction payments,—
as that term is used in the Income Tax Act 2004, from which P, P’s employer, or any other relevant person must withhold an amount of tax under the PAYE rules as defined in that Act:
as that term is used in the Income Tax Act 1994, from which P, P’s employer, or any other relevant person must make a tax deduction under the PAYE rules as defined in that Act:
as that term is used in the Income Tax Act 1976, from which P, P’s employer, or any other relevant person must make a tax deduction under Part 11 of that Act:
as that term is used in the Income Tax Assessment Act 1957, from which P, P’s employer, or any other relevant person must make a tax deduction under Part 2 of that Act.

This regulation applies in respect of a period of employment, whether or not P’s employer (or any other relevant person) failed to pay an amount of tax deducted or withheld to the Commissioner of Inland Revenue, if MSD is satisfied that—
P’s employer (or any other relevant person) deducted or withheld tax from payments made under subclause (1); and
P did not contribute to the employer’s (or any other relevant person’s) failure to pay that tax to the Commissioner.
For the purposes of satisfying the residential requirement for any benefit after the return to New Zealand, on or after 23 June 1987, of P or the spouse or partner or any child of P,—
P must be treated as being resident and present in New Zealand during the period to which this regulation applies:

if the spouse or partner or any child of P was with P during that period or any part of it, the spouse or partner or child must be treated as being resident and present in New Zealand during that period or that part of it, as the case may be:
any child of P born outside New Zealand during that period must be treated as having been born in New Zealand.
Nothing in subclause (3) may be construed to derogate from section 219 of the Act, or any regulations referred to in that section.

prov.label: 7A
prov.heading: Circumstances in which certain holders of, or people who would have been eligible for, Christchurch response visa are taken to meet residential requirement

para.text: A person to whom this regulation applies is taken to meet the residential requirement for a benefit.
This regulation applies to a person who, on or after 3 June 2019,—
is not a New Zealand citizen, but holds a residence class visa that is a Christchurch response visa under the Immigration Act 2009; and
is ordinarily resident in New Zealand when the person first applies for the benefit; and
has not resided continuously in New Zealand for a period of at least 2 years at any one time after becoming a resident.
This regulation also applies to a person who, on or after 3 June 2019,—
is a New Zealand citizen or holds a residence class visa (other than a Christchurch response visa) under the Immigration Act 2009; and

would have been eligible for a Christchurch response visa if the person was not a New Zealand citizen or did not hold a residence class visa (other than a Christchurch response visa) under the Immigration Act 2009; and
is ordinarily resident in New Zealand when the person first applies for the benefit; and
has not resided continuously in New Zealand for a period of at least 2 years at any one time after becoming a citizen or resident.
In this regulation, Christchurch response visa means a permanent resident visa of a kind described in the following immigration instructions:
RV2.16 (Permanent resident visas for residents affected by terrorist attacks on 15 March 2019); and
S4.30 (Christchurch Response (2019) Category).
In this regulation, immigration instructions has the same meaning as in section 4 of the Immigration Act 2009, and a reference in subclause (4) to a specified immigration instruction—
is one to it as at 3 June 2019 and as published by the chief executive of the department responsible for the administration of that Act; and
includes a reference to any later immigration instruction that, with or without modification, replaces, or that corresponds to, it.

history.history-note: Regulation 7A: inserted, on 3 June 2019, by regulation 5 of the Social Security (Residential Requirement—Christchurch Response Visa) Amendment Regulations 2019 (LI 2019/115).

subpart.heading: Jobseeker Support: certificates and medical examinations

prov.heading: Certificates: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is any of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 27 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a dentist:
a midwife:
a nurse practitioner.

prov.heading: Medical examinations: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is any of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 28 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a psychologist:
a nurse practitioner.

prov.heading: Minimum period of eligibility

para.text: For the purposes of section 35(2)﻿(a) of the Act, the minimum period of time for which a person’s restricting health condition, injury, or disability must be expected to continue is 2 years.

prov.heading: Certificates and medical examinations: prescribed health practitioners

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Restricted work capacity or total blindness: medical examination

para.text: A health practitioner who is any of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 37 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a psychologist:
a nurse practitioner.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Caring for another person: certificate about person cared for

para.text: A health practitioner who is either of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 41 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a nurse practitioner.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Caring for another person: medical examination of person cared for

para.text: A health practitioner who is either of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 42 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a nurse practitioner.

subpart.heading: Youth payment: criteria for incentive payments

prov.heading: When young person meets criteria for education, training, or work-based learning incentive payment

para.text: A young person meets the criteria for the payment of an education, training, or work-based learning incentive payment (for the purpose of sections 55 and 62 of the Act) if—
the young person is receiving a youth payment or a young parent payment; and
the young person has, since starting to receive a youth support payment, completed 6 months’ participation and achievement in education, training, or work-based learning, as the case may be; and
the contracted service provider in question has confirmed to MSD, or, as the case requires, MSD is satisfied, that the young person has complied with paragraph (b).

prov.heading: When young person meets criteria for budgeting incentive payment

para.text: A young person meets the criteria for the payment of a budgeting incentive payment (for the purpose of sections 55 and 62 of the Act) if—
the young person is receiving a youth payment or a young parent payment; and

the young person has, since starting to receive a youth support payment, completed 3 continuous months’ attendance and participation in discussions on budgeting with the person’s contracted service provider or, as the case requires, MSD; and
the young person has completed a budgeting programme approved by MSD for the purposes of section 162(1)﻿(b) of the Act; and
the service provider has confirmed to MSD, or, as the case requires, MSD is satisfied, that the young person has complied with paragraph (b).

prov.heading: When young person meets criteria for parenting education incentive payment

para.text: A young person meets the criteria for the payment of a parenting education incentive payment (for the purpose of sections 55 and 62 of the Act) if—
the young person is receiving a young parent payment; and

the young person has, since starting to receive a youth support payment, completed 3 continuous months’ regular engagement with the person’s contracted service provider or, as the case requires, MSD; and
the service provider has confirmed to MSD, or, as the case requires, MSD is satisfied, that the young person has complied with paragraph (b); and
the young person has participated in and completed a parenting education programme approved by MSD for the purposes of section 164(1)﻿(a) of the Act; and
each of the young person’s dependent children is enrolled with a primary health organisation; and
each of the young person’s dependent children aged 5 years or below is up to date with checks under the WellChild programme or any similar programme established in its place; and

each of the young person’s dependent children aged 5 years or below attends an approved early childhood education programme or is placed in other suitable childcare during any time that the young person spends in education, training, work-based learning, or part-time work.

If a young person is unable to comply with the requirements of any of paragraphs (d) to (g) of subclause (1) because of the unavailability of the programme, service, or enrolment in question, the young person must be treated as complying if the young person takes steps agreed with the young person’s contracted service provider or, as the case requires, with MSD in preparation for the availability of the programme, service, or enrolment (for example, enrolling on a waiting list).

prov.heading: Assets requirement

para.text: The assets requirement, for the purposes of section 65(1)﻿(b) of the Act, is that a person’s cash assets must not exceed—
$16,200, in the case of—
a person who is in a relationship; or
a single person who has a dependent child or children:
$8,100, in any other case.
This regulation is subject to section 69(2), (2A), and (2B) of the Act.

Under section 69(2) of the Act, for the purposes of the assets requirement in this regulation and of section 69 of the Act, the assets of an applicant for an accommodation supplement (A) include the assets of A’s spouse or partner (if any).
Under section 69(2A) of the Act, section 69(2) of the Act applies, without limitation, even if—
A is a community spouse or partner (as defined in Schedule 2 of the Act); and
A’s spouse or partner is receiving long-term residential care in a hospital or rest home.

Under section 69(2B) of the Act, section 69(2) of the Act does not limit section 67(c) of the Act, under which a person (P, who may, or may not, be A’s spouse or partner) is ineligible for an accommodation supplement if P is a resident assessed as requiring care in respect of whom a funder is paying some or all of the cost of contracted care services under section 54, 55, 56, 57, or 58 of the Residential Care and Disability Support Services Act 2018.
Subclauses (3) to (5) are by way of explanation only. They do not affect the provisions specified in them.

history.history-note: Regulation 15(2): replaced, on 26 November 2022, by section 13 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 15(3): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 13 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 15(4): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 13 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 15(5): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 13 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 15(6): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 13 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).

prov.heading: Cash assets exemptions

para.text: A person’s cash assets, under sections 68(2)﻿(c) and 423(1)﻿(b) of the Act, exclude any amount, item, or kind of cash assets, declared by Part 1 of Schedule 8 of these regulations not to be cash assets.

prov.cf: 1964 No 136 s 61E(1), definition of cash assets, paragraph (b); SR 2011/287

prov.heading: Base rate

para.text: In this regulation,—
beneficiary means a person who is being paid—
a main benefit; or
New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension
benefit, in subclause (2), means a benefit referred to in paragraph (a) or (b) of the definition in this subclause of beneficiary
non-beneficiary means a person who is not a beneficiary (as defined in this regulation).
The base rate is as follows:

for a single beneficiary under the age of 25 years, the maximum weekly rate of a benefit that the beneficiary would have been entitled to receive, before any abatement or deduction, if the beneficiary had attained the age of 25 years:
for a single beneficiary with 1 or more dependent children,—
the maximum weekly rate of a benefit that the beneficiary is entitled to receive, before any abatement or deduction; plus

the maximum annual rate of family tax credit (divided by 52) that is payable in respect of an eldest dependent child who is under 16 years old under subparts MA to MF and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007:
for any other single beneficiary, the maximum weekly rate of a benefit that the beneficiary would be entitled to receive before any abatement or deduction:
for a beneficiary who is in a relationship and is not a beneficiary to whom paragraph (e) or (ea) applies,—
the maximum weekly rate of a benefit that the beneficiary is entitled to receive, before any abatement or deduction; plus

if the beneficiary has 1 or more dependent children, the maximum annual rate of family tax credit (divided by 52) that is paid in respect of an eldest dependent child who is under 16 years (if any) under subparts MA to MF and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007; plus
the maximum weekly rate of a benefit paid in respect of the beneficiary’s spouse or partner:

for a beneficiary who is in a relationship and whose spouse or partner is not entitled to an accommodation supplement under section 230 of the Act, the rate specified in paragraph (d) as if a rate of a benefit were paid in respect of that spouse or partner:

for a beneficiary who is a community spouse or partner (as defined in Schedule 2 of the Act), the base rate that would apply to them under paragraph (a), (b), or (c) if they were single (and their other circumstances were unchanged):
for a single non-beneficiary with 1 or more dependent children,—
the appropriate maximum weekly rate of jobseeker support; plus
the annual rate of family tax credit (divided by 52) that is payable in respect of an eldest dependent child who is under 16 years under subparts MA to MF and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007:
for any other single non-beneficiary, the weekly rate of jobseeker support at the rate in clause 1(d) of Part 1 of Schedule 4 of the Act before any abatement or deduction:
for a non-beneficiary who is in a relationship and is not a non-beneficiary to whom paragraph (i) applies,—
the appropriate maximum weekly rate of jobseeker support; plus

if the beneficiary has 1 or more dependent children, the maximum annual rate of family tax credit (divided by 52) that is payable in respect of an eldest dependent child who is under 16 years under subparts MA to MF and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007; plus
the maximum weekly rate of jobseeker support that would have been payable in respect of the beneficiary’s spouse or partner:

for a non-beneficiary who is a community spouse or partner (as defined in Schedule 2 of the Act), the base rate that would apply to them under paragraph (f) or (g) if they were single (and their other circumstances were unchanged).

cf.citation: 1964 No 136 s 61E(1), definitions of beneficiary and non-beneficiary, Schedule 18, Part 1, cl 1, definition of base rate, cl 4A

history.history-note: Regulation 17(2)﻿(d): amended, on 26 November 2022, by section 14(1) of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 17(2)﻿(ea): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 14(2) of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 17(2)﻿(h): amended, on 26 November 2022, by section 14(3) of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 17(2)﻿(i): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 14(4) of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).

prov.heading: Income-based reductions to assessed base rate for non-beneficiary

para.text: This regulation applies to the amount of accommodation supplement assessed—
under subpart 10 of Part 2 and Part 7 of Schedule 4 of the Act and these regulations; and
for a non-beneficiary (as defined in regulation 17).
That amount must be reduced by 25 cents for every $1 of the relevant weekly income (see subclause (2A)) in excess of the income level specified in subclause (3).
The relevant weekly income mentioned in subclause (2) is,—
if the applicant is not a community spouse or partner (as defined in Schedule 2 of the Act), the combined weekly income of—
the applicant; and
any spouse or partner of the applicant; and

if the applicant is a community spouse or partner (as so defined), the weekly income of the applicant alone (disregarding any income of the applicant’s spouse or partner who is receiving long-term residential care in a hospital or rest home).
The income level mentioned in subclause (2) is the amount of income that would prevent payment of jobseeker support,—
for a single applicant without dependent children, at the maximum rate in clause 1(d) of Part 1 of Schedule 4 of the Act; or
for a sole parent, at the appropriate maximum rate in clause 1(e) or (f) of Part 1 of Schedule 4 of the Act as if Income Test 3 applied to that rate instead of Income Test 1; or
for any other applicant, at the appropriate maximum rate in Part 1 of Schedule 4 of the Act.

cf.citation: 1964 No 136 s 61E(1), definitions of beneficiary and non-beneficiary, Schedule 18, Part 1, cl 2

history.history-note: Regulation 18(2): replaced, on 26 November 2022, by section 15 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).
Regulation 18(2A): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 15 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).

prov.heading: Rounding

para.text: This regulation applies to every accommodation supplement assessed under subpart 10 of Part 2 and Part 7 of Schedule 4 of the Act and these regulations.
The accommodation supplement must be rounded up to the complete dollar.

subpart.heading: Childcare assistance

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In regulations 20 to 48, unless the context otherwise requires,—
applicant means a person who has applied for childcare assistance under regulation 29, and includes a person to whom childcare assistance has been granted
approved activity—
in regulations 30 to 37, means an activity listed in regulation 33; and
in regulations 38 to 44, means an activity listed in regulation 40
approved early childhood education programme means a programme of early childhood education that—
is provided by any of the following (within the meaning of section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020):
a licensed early childhood service:
a playgroup that is a certified playgroup under the Education (Playgroups) Regulations 2008; and
charges a uniform monetary fee for the participation of children in the programme
childcare assistance means a childcare subsidy or an OSCAR subsidy
childcare subsidy means the subsidy of the kind provided for in regulations 30 to 37
cohort entry policy, in relation to a school, means a cohort entry policy adopted by the school under section 63 of the Education and Training Act 2020
educator has the meaning given in section 19(3) of the Education and Training Act 2020
eligible child—
in regulations 30 to 37 means a child for whom a childcare subsidy may be paid by virtue of regulation 30; and
in regulations 38 to 44 means a child for whom an OSCAR subsidy may be paid by virtue of regulation 38
household income—
in relation to a child or the child’s principal caregiver, means the sum of—
the total income of the child’s principal caregiver; and
the total income of the spouse or partner (if any) of the child’s principal caregiver (whether or not that spouse or partner is also the child’s other caregiver); or
in relation to a child or the child’s temporary OB or UCB caregiver, means the sum of—
the total income of the child’s temporary OB or UCB caregiver; and
the total income of the spouse or partner (if any) of the child’s temporary OB or UCB caregiver (whether or not that spouse or partner is also the child’s other caregiver)
licensed home-based education and care service has the meaning given in section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020
OSCAR programme—
means a programme for children’s out-of-school care and recreation approved under regulation 45; and
subject to clause 1 of Schedule 1, includes a programme covered by an approval continued by clause 1 of Schedule 1
OSCAR subsidy (except in the definition in this subclause of OSCAR programme) means the out-of-school care and recreation subsidy provided for in regulations 38 to 47
other caregiver, in relation to a child, means a person who—
lives together with the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver; and
is a parent, step-parent, or person having the responsibilities of a parent, of the child
serious disability or illness,—

in relation to deciding whether a person should be granted childcare assistance because the person, or some other person, has a serious disability or illness, means a physical, medical, psychological, or psychiatric condition that—
reduces the person’s or the other person’s (as the case may be) independent function; and
is likely to continue for more than 6 months; but

in relation to deciding whether childcare assistance granted to a person because the person, or some other person, has a serious disability or illness should continue to be paid, means a physical, medical, psychological, or psychiatric condition that reduces the person’s or the other person’s (as the case may be) independent function
tertiary educational institution means an institution within the meaning of section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020
total income, in relation to a child’s caregiver, means the sum of—

the caregiver’s income as defined in Part 2 of Schedule 3 of the Act (including, without limitation, a payment that is weekly income of the caregiver under clauses 3 and 8 and Part 3A of Schedule 3 of the Act because it is an information share child support payment); and
a child support excess payment, which includes, without limitation, a mixed child support payment (see clause 8(na) of Schedule 3 of the Act); and
the amount of any benefit received by the caregiver other than any of the following:

any benefit received by the caregiver on or after 26 November 2017 that is childcare assistance, a child disability allowance, an orphan’s benefit, an unsupported child’s benefit, or a winter energy payment:

any benefit received by the caregiver on or after 1 July 2021 that is an accommodation supplement, temporary additional support, a special benefit, a disability allowance, a special disability allowance, or an incentive payment; but
does not include the following allowances under Part 4 or 5 of Schedule 4 of the Act:
a clothing allowance received by the caregiver on or after 1 July 2018; or
a holiday allowance or birthday allowance received by the caregiver on or after 1 December 2021
week means a period of 7 days commencing at midnight on a Sunday.

For the purposes of the definition of approved early childhood education programme, the provider of the programme charges a uniform monetary fee for children to participate in the programme even if, in the case of families with 2 or more children, it charges for a second or further child a uniform fee that is less than the uniform fee charged for a first or an only child.

history.history-note: Regulation 20(1) approved early childhood education programme paragraph (a): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).
Regulation 20(1) approved early childhood education programme paragraph (a)﻿(ii): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).
Regulation 20(1) cohort entry policy: amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).
Regulation 20(1) educator: inserted, on 3 June 2024, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Childcare Subsidy) Amendment Regulations 2024 (SL 2024/50).
Regulation 20(1) household income: replaced, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 20(1) licensed home-based education and care service: inserted, on 3 June 2024, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Childcare Subsidy) Amendment Regulations 2024 (SL 2024/50).
Regulation 20(1) other caregiver paragraph (a): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 20(1) tertiary educational institution: amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).
Regulation 20(1) total income paragraph (a): replaced, on 1 July 2023, by section 51(1) of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).
Regulation 20(1) total income paragraph (aa): inserted, on 1 July 2023, by section 51(1) of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).
Regulation 20(1) total income paragraph (b): replaced, on 1 July 2021, by regulation 4 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2021 (LI 2021/121).
Regulation 20(1) total income paragraph (b)﻿(ii): amended, on 1 July 2023, by section 51(2) of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).
Regulation 20(1) total income paragraph (c): revoked, on 1 July 2023, by section 51(3) of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).
Regulation 20(1) total income paragraph (d): inserted, on 1 July 2021, by section 10(2) of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Child not eligible for both childcare subsidy and OSCAR subsidy

para.text: In relation to any period, payments may be made under a childcare subsidy granted for a child or an OSCAR subsidy granted for that child, but not under both.

prov.heading: Hours to be calculated weekly

para.text: The hours during which an eligible child participates in an approved early childhood education programme or OSCAR programme must be calculated by the week.

prov.heading: Childcare assistance not payable in respect of certain hours

para.text: Childcare assistance is not payable in respect of any hour of a child’s participation in an approved early childhood education programme or OSCAR programme if, for that hour, payment other than childcare assistance is made towards the costs of the child’s participation in the programme through a funding programme—
that is provided by the Government in order to purchase hours of childcare; and
under which the amount provided per hour for childcare exceeds the hourly rate of childcare assistance as calculated under regulation 24.

However, MSD must pay a childcare subsidy in respect of any hour of an eligible child's participation in an approved early childhood education programme where the costs of participating in the programme, for that hour, are funded by government funding other than childcare assistance if—
the programme is provided by a licensed home-based education and care service; and
the educator requires a top-up payment to ensure the educator’s hourly rate of providing education and care in relation to the programme is covered.

history.history-note: Regulation 23(2): inserted, on 3 June 2024, by regulation 5 of the Social Security (Childcare Subsidy) Amendment Regulations 2024 (SL 2024/50).

prov.heading: Assistance to be no more than hourly fee payable

para.text: The hourly rate of childcare assistance paid in respect of a child’s participation in an approved early childhood education programme or OSCAR programme must be—
no more than the actual hourly fee payable (or, if subclause (3) applies, the averaged hourly fee payable) for that participation; or
if regulation 28 applies, the higher of the fee charged for keeping open the child’s place in the programme and the hourly fee calculated under paragraph (a).

For the purposes of subclause (1), the actual hourly fee payable for a child’s participation in a programme does not include any payment made by a person other than the applicant towards the fee payable in respect of that child’s participation in the programme.

If a periodic flat fee is payable for a child’s participation in a programme, or for keeping open a child’s place in a programme, the averaged hourly fee payable for the purposes of subclause (1) is the amount obtained by—
subtracting from that flat fee any payment made by a person other than the applicant towards the flat fee; and
dividing the amount calculated under paragraph (a) by the number of hours during the period concerned for which the child is enrolled to participate in the programme.
This regulation is subject to regulation 23.

prov.heading: Assistance may continue if caregiver not working because of health condition, injury, or disability

para.text: Childcare assistance may continue to be paid for up to 12 weeks of any period when the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of the child concerned (or, if applicable, the child’s other caregiver) is not working if—
the assistance would not otherwise be payable because the caregiver is not working; but
the caregiver is unable to work because of a health condition, an injury, or a disability.

history.history-note: Regulation 25: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Assistance may continue if caregiver changes employment

para.text: Childcare assistance may continue to be paid for up to 10 working days before the start of the new employment concerned if—

the assistance has been granted on the basis of the employment of the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of the child concerned (or, if applicable, of the child’s other caregiver); and
the employment has ended but the caregiver has arranged new employment.

history.history-note: Regulation 26(a): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Absence of child to be notified

para.text: Within a reasonable time after a child for whom childcare assistance is payable is absent from the early childhood education programme or OSCAR programme concerned, the applicant must notify MSD of the child’s absence.

prov.heading: Payment of assistance during child’s absence

para.text: Childcare assistance may continue to be paid for the period set out in subclauses (2) and (3) during a child’s absence from an early childhood education programme or OSCAR programme—
if the absence is due to COVID-19 requirements, and whether or not the programme charges a fee for holding open the places of children who are absent; or
if the absence is due to the child’s medical condition, or is due to any other reason, and the programme charges a fee for holding open the places of children who are absent.
The period is calculated as—
commencing on the first day of the child’s absence from the programme; and
including only the time during the absence when the programme is available.
The period must not exceed—
the weeks of the absence, if the absence is due to COVID-19 requirements; or
6 weeks, if the absence is due to the child’s medical condition; or
3 weeks, if the absence is due to any other reason.
In this regulation,—
available, for the programme, if subclause (3)﻿(aa) applies to the child, means that—
the programme is attended by, and provided to, any other child, or any other children, to whom COVID-19 requirements do not apply; or
the programme is not attended by, nor provided to, any children, because their absence is due to reasons that are or include COVID-19 requirements
COVID-19 requirements, for a child or children, means requirements that apply to the child or children, and that are imposed—
by or under an Act; and
to prevent, or limit the risk of, the outbreak or spread of COVID-19.

history.history-note: Regulation 28(1): replaced, on 25 August 2020, by regulation 4(1) of the Social Security (COVID-19—Childcare Assistance) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/223).
Regulation 28(3)﻿(aa): inserted, on 25 August 2020, by regulation 4(2) of the Social Security (COVID-19—Childcare Assistance) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/223).
Regulation 28(4): inserted, on 25 August 2020, by regulation 4(3) of the Social Security (COVID-19—Childcare Assistance) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/223).

prov.heading: Applications and evidence

para.text: No person can be granted a childcare subsidy or an OSCAR subsidy unless the person—
has applied for it on a form in writing provided by MSD for the purpose and completed in writing by or on behalf of the applicant (see also regulations 166(4)﻿(f) and 172); and
has given MSD the evidence MSD requires to satisfy MSD that the person is eligible for the subsidy.

subpart.crosshead: Childcare subsidy

prov.heading: Eligibility of child for payment of childcare subsidy

para.text: A childcare subsidy may be paid for a child only if the child is a dependent child—
who is no older than 5 years and 28 days, and is not yet attending school; or

who is 5 years old, whose parent, principal caregiver, temporary OB or UCB caregiver, or guardian intends to enrol the child in a school that has a cohort entry policy in place, and who (under section 64(2) of the Education and Training Act 2020) may be enrolled in that school not earlier than the child’s fifth birthday on a date that is a term start date or a mid-term start date; or
who is under 6 years old, and for whom a child disability allowance is payable.
Eligibility under subclause (1)﻿(b) ends on the first term start date or mid-term start date to occur on or after the child’s fifth birthday.
In this regulation, mid-term start date and term start date have the meanings given in section 5B of the Education Act 1989.

history.history-note: Regulation 30(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 30(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).
Regulation 30(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 January 2020, by regulation 4(1) of the Social Security (Childcare Assistance—Cohort Entry Policy) Amendment Regulations 2019 (LI 2019/264).
Regulation 30(2): replaced, on 1 January 2020, by regulation 4(2) of the Social Security (Childcare Assistance—Cohort Entry Policy) Amendment Regulations 2019 (LI 2019/264).
Regulation 30(3): inserted, on 1 January 2020, by regulation 4(2) of the Social Security (Childcare Assistance—Cohort Entry Policy) Amendment Regulations 2019 (LI 2019/264).

prov.heading: Childcare subsidy available only if payable under regulation 32, 34, or 35

para.text: A childcare subsidy for an eligible child’s participation in approved early childhood education programmes is payable only if regulation 32, 34, or 35 provides that it is payable.

prov.heading: Payment of childcare subsidy if caregiver engaged in approved activity

para.text: A childcare subsidy for an eligible child’s participation in approved early childhood education programmes is payable for periods of a total of up to 50 hours a week if the child is participating in (or will participate in) 1 or more approved early childhood education programmes for a total of 3 or more hours a week, and—
subclause (2) applies to the child, and either subclause (3) or (4) applies to each of those periods; or
a child disability allowance is payable for the child; or

the child’s principal caregiver is also the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of some other child, who is either in hospital or is a child for whom a child disability allowance is payable; or

the child’s temporary OB or UCB caregiver is also the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of some other child, who is either in hospital or is a child for whom a child disability allowance is payable.
This subclause applies to a child if at least 1 of the following applies:
the child has no other caregiver:
the child’s other caregiver is engaged in an approved activity:
the child’s other caregiver is not engaged in an approved activity, but MSD is satisfied that for some good reason the other caregiver cannot care for the child.
This subclause applies to a period if, during that period,—
the child is participating in an approved early childhood education programme; and

the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver is engaged in an approved activity or travelling between the place where the programme is provided and the place where the activity is engaged in.

This subclause applies to a period if the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver is engaged in shift employment and (whether the child is participating in an approved early childhood education programme then, or participates in such a programme at other times when the caregiver usually sleeps), during that period, the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver—
is undertaking that employment; or
is travelling between the place where that employment is engaged in and—
an approved early childhood education programme that the child is participating in; or
the caregiver’s home.

history.history-note: Regulation 32 heading: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 32(1)﻿(c): replaced, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 32(1)﻿(d): inserted, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 32(3)﻿(b): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 32(4): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Activities in respect of which childcare subsidy available under regulation 32

para.text: A childcare subsidy for a child is available under regulation 32 only to enable the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver or other caregiver to undertake one of the following:
employment (whether full-time or part-time, and whether permanent, temporary, or casual):
a rehabilitation programme approved by MSD:
any activity specified in section 146(1)﻿(d) of the Act that the caregiver is required to undertake under that section or is undertaking under section 324 of the Act:
an employment-related training course offered by a course-provider accredited by the New Zealand Qualifications Authority:
any other employment-related training course approved by MSD:
a course of study at a secondary school (within the meaning of section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020) or a tertiary educational institution.

history.history-note: Regulation 33: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 33(f): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

prov.heading: Payment of childcare subsidy if caregiver not engaged in approved activity but has serious disability or illness

para.text: A childcare subsidy for a child’s participation in approved early childhood education programmes is payable for up to 50 hours a week if—
the child is participating in (or will participate in) 1 or more approved early childhood education programmes for a total of 3 or more hours a week; and
MSD is satisfied, in the light of a certificate from a medical practitioner or a nurse practitioner, that the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver—
has a serious disability or illness; and
needs childcare for the child for more than 9 hours a week; and
at least 1 of the following applies:
the child has no other caregiver:
the child’s other caregiver is engaged in an approved activity:
the child’s other caregiver is not engaged in an approved activity, but MSD is satisfied that for some good reason the other caregiver cannot care for the child:
a child disability allowance is payable for the child.

history.history-note: Regulation 34 heading: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 34(b): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Payment of childcare subsidy if caregiver neither engaged in approved activity nor has serious disability or illness

para.text: A childcare subsidy for an eligible child’s participation in approved early childhood education programmes is payable for up to 9 hours a week if—
the child is participating in (or will participate in) 1 or more approved early childhood education programmes for a total of 3 or more hours a week; and
the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver is not engaged in an approved activity; and
regulation 34 does not apply.

history.history-note: Regulation 35 heading: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 35(b): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Rate of childcare subsidy

para.text: A childcare subsidy is payable to a person at the appropriate rate stated in Schedule 2.

Subclause (1) is subject to regulation 24 (which provides that the hourly rate of childcare assistance paid in respect of a child’s participation in an approved early childhood education programme must be no more than the actual hourly fee payable).

prov.heading: Childcare subsidy usually payable to service

para.text: A childcare subsidy in respect of an approved early childhood education programme must be paid to—
the service providing the programme; or
the service that arranged the scheme under which the programme is provided.
Subclause (1) does not prevent MSD from paying a lump sum of childcare subsidy directly to a child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver if—

the sum represents an underpayment of the amounts that should have been paid to a provider of a licensed home-based education and care service in respect of the child’s participation in that service; and
the caregiver has already paid to the service or arranger the amount of the underpayment.

history.history-note: Regulation 37(2): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

subpart.crosshead: Out-of-school care and recreation subsidy—OSCAR subsidy

prov.heading: Eligibility of child for payment of OSCAR subsidy

para.text: An OSCAR subsidy may be paid for a child only if the child—
is a dependent child; and
is participating in (or will participate in) 1 or more OSCAR programmes for a total of 3 or more hours a week; and
is—
at least 5, and under 14; or
14 or older, and is a child for whom a child disability allowance is payable.

history.history-note: Regulation 38(c)﻿(ii): revoked, on 1 January 2020, by regulation 5 of the Social Security (Childcare Assistance—Cohort Entry Policy) Amendment Regulations 2019 (LI 2019/264).

prov.heading: OSCAR subsidy available only if payable under regulation 41 or 42

para.text: An OSCAR subsidy for an eligible child’s participation in an OSCAR programme is payable only if regulation 41 or 42 provides that it is payable.

prov.heading: Activities in respect of which OSCAR subsidy available under regulation 41

para.text: An OSCAR subsidy for a child is available under regulation 41 only to enable the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver to undertake one of the following:
employment (whether full-time or part-time, and whether permanent, temporary, or casual):
any activity specified in section 146(1)﻿(d) of the Act that the caregiver is required to undertake under that section or is undertaking under section 324 of the Act:
an employment-related training course offered by a course-provider accredited by the New Zealand Qualifications Authority:
a course of study at a secondary school (within the meaning of section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020) or a tertiary educational institution.

history.history-note: Regulation 40: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 40(d): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

prov.heading: Payment of OSCAR subsidy if caregiver engaged in approved activity

para.text: An OSCAR subsidy for an eligible child’s participation in OSCAR programmes is payable for periods of a total of up to 20 hours a week during the school term, or up to 50 hours a week during the school holidays, if, and only if,—
subclause (2) applies to the child, and either subclause (3) or (4) applies to each of those periods; or
a child disability allowance is payable for the child; or

the child’s principal caregiver is also the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of some other child, who is either in hospital or is a child for whom a child disability allowance is payable; or

the child’s temporary OB or UCB caregiver is also the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of some other child, who is either in hospital or is a child for whom a child disability allowance is payable.
This subclause applies to a child if at least 1 of the following applies:
the child has no other caregiver:
the child’s other caregiver is engaged in an approved activity:
the child’s other caregiver is not engaged in an approved activity, but MSD is satisfied that for some good reason the other caregiver cannot care for the child.
This subclause applies to a period if, during that period,—
the child is participating in an OSCAR programme; and

the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver is engaged in an approved activity or travelling between the place where the programme is participated in and the place where the activity is engaged in.

This subclause applies to a period if the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver is engaged in shift employment and (whether the child is participating in an OSCAR programme then, or participates in such a programme at other times when the caregiver usually sleeps), during that period, the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver—
is undertaking that employment; or
is travelling between the place where that employment is engaged in and—
a place where the child is participating in an OSCAR programme; or
the caregiver’s home.

history.history-note: Regulation 41 heading: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 41(1)﻿(c): replaced, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 41(1)﻿(d): inserted, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 41(3)﻿(b): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 41(4): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Payment of OSCAR subsidy if caregiver has serious disability or illness

para.text: An OSCAR subsidy for a child’s participation in an OSCAR programme is payable for up to 20 hours a week during the school term, or up to 50 hours a week during the school holidays, if—
the child is participating in (or will participate in) 1 or more OSCAR programmes for a total of 3 or more hours a week; and
MSD is satisfied, in the light of a certificate from a medical practitioner or a nurse practitioner, that the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver—
has a serious disability or illness; and
needs an OSCAR programme for the child for more than 3 hours a week; and
at least 1 of the following applies:
the child has no other caregiver:
the child’s other caregiver is engaged in an approved activity:
the child’s other caregiver is not engaged in an approved activity, but MSD is satisfied that for some good reason the other caregiver cannot care for the child:
a child disability allowance is payable for the child.

history.history-note: Regulation 42 heading: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 42(b): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: OSCAR subsidy payable to provider or caregiver

para.text: An OSCAR subsidy may be paid to the provider of the OSCAR programme concerned, or to the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of the child concerned.

history.history-note: Regulation 43 heading: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 43: amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Rate of OSCAR subsidy

para.text: An OSCAR subsidy is payable to a person at the appropriate rate stated in Schedule 2.

subpart.crosshead: Approval of OSCAR programmes

prov.heading: Approval of programmes

para.text: MSD, if satisfied, in the light of the matters stated in regulation 47, that an out-of-school programme or proposed out-of-school programme is or will be properly run, may, by written notice to the provider, approve it for the purposes of this Part.
An approval—
may be unconditional or subject to conditions stated in the notice; and
may be for an indefinite period or for a period stated in the notice.

prov.heading: Suspension and revocation of approvals

para.text: MSD may, by written notice to its provider, suspend or revoke the approval of a programme under regulation 45—
if MSD is satisfied, in the light of the matters stated in regulation 47, that it is not properly run; or
if, after making reasonable efforts to do so, MSD has been unable to obtain enough information to be satisfied, in the light of the matters stated in regulation 47, that it is properly run; or
if MSD is satisfied that there has been a breach of a condition subject to which the approval was given.

prov.heading: Matters to be considered before programmes approved or approvals suspended or revoked

para.text: The following matters must be considered in deciding whether an out-of-school programme is properly run (or a proposed out-of-school programme will be properly run):
the adequacy with which the programme provides for—
the health and safety of the children participating:
the care and recreation of the children participating:
the health and safety of the staff and volunteers involved:
the competence, training, and suitability of the staff and volunteers involved in the programme:
the competence of the programme’s supervision:
the suitability and safety of the environment in which the programme is operating:
the competence of the provider’s management (including its financial management):
the extent of the programme’s compliance with relevant enactments and bylaws:
the programme’s responsiveness to applicable cultural issues.

subpart.crosshead: Miscellaneous: overpayments

prov.heading: Recovery of overpayments

para.text: A payment of childcare assistance that is an overpayment (within the meaning of regulation 206(f)) is—
a debt due to the Crown for the purposes of section 353(a) of the Act from the applicant; and
subject to recovery under section 362(1) (MSD’s duty to recover debts) of the Act from the applicant.

If, because a child has ceased to be enrolled or has been absent from an approved early childhood education programme or an OSCAR programme, childcare assistance has been paid to a service in excess of the amount that the service was entitled to be paid, MSD may recover the amount of the excess by making 1 or more deductions from any other amount or amounts payable to the service under these regulations.

subpart.heading: Child disability allowance: certificates and medical examinations

prov.heading: Certificates and medical examinations: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is either of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of sections 80 and 81 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a nurse practitioner.

prov.heading: Discretionary grant if life expectancy or continuing disability less than 6 months

para.text: This regulation specifies a special category of eligibility for the purposes of section 85(1)﻿(b) of the Act.
MSD may grant a disability allowance to a person who would otherwise be eligible for it but—
whose life expectancy is less than 6 months; or
in respect of whom it is not possible to determine whether the disability is likely to continue for at least 6 months but that is a reasonably possible prognosis.

prov.heading: Mandatory grant if disability unexpectedly likely to last, or lasts, for at least 6 months

para.text: This regulation specifies a special category of eligibility for the purposes of section 85(1)﻿(b) and (3) of the Act.

This regulation applies if a person whose application for a disability allowance has been declined only on the ground that the disability is likely to last less than 6 months later satisfies MSD that—
the disability is likely to last, or has continued, for 6 months or more; and
the person would otherwise have been eligible to receive a disability allowance from the date of the original application.
MSD must grant a disability allowance to the person commencing on the date of the original application.

prov.heading: Medical examinations: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is either of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 88 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a nurse practitioner.

prov.heading: Funeral grants: methods of payment: people to whom payment may be made

para.text: A payment made under section 90 of the Act must be made, in MSD’s discretion,—
to the spouse or partner or any child of the deceased; or
to the estate of the deceased; or
to any person who has paid the deceased’s funeral expenses; or
to the appropriate funeral director; or
in the case of a deceased child, to the parent or any other person or persons who were liable in law to maintain that child immediately before the date of death.

prov.heading: Funeral grants: methods of payment: conditional payment

para.text: This regulation applies if, due to 1 or both of the following, there is uncertainty about whether a funeral grant is payable under section 90 of the Act:

a deceased person’s estate (including, without limitation, any assets of any person from which the deceased person’s funeral expenses are under section 91 or 93 of the Act to be paid) is yet to be determined:
the income of a person whose income is to be tested under section 91 or 93 of the Act has not yet been determined.
MSD may pay a funeral grant on the condition that the whole or part of it will be repaid if it is later established that there was no eligibility for all or any part of that payment.
A funeral grant payment to be repaid under subclause (2), because it is later established that there was no eligibility for all or any part of that payment, is—
a debt due to the Crown for the purposes of section 353(a) of the Act from the person to whom the funeral grant payment was made; and
subject to recovery under section 362(1) (MSD’s duty to recover debts) of the Act.

prov.heading: Funeral grants: amounts

para.text: A funeral grant under section 90 of the Act is of an amount, not exceeding $2,697.43, that MSD determines is appropriate to meet the deceased person’s reasonable funeral expenses.

history.history-note: Regulation 55: amended, on 1 April 2026, by regulation 4 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations 2026 (SL 2026/37).

subpart.heading: Temporary additional support
subpart.crosshead: Criteria and requirements for temporary additional support

prov.heading: Overview of this subpart

para.text: This subpart—
defines the kinds of income, benefits, credits, and receipts that are chargeable income; and
defines the kinds of costs that are allowable costs; and
prescribes amounts allowed for standard living costs that ought to be met from chargeable income (that is, amounts defined as standard costs); and
defines the kinds of assets that are cash assets, and prescribes an amount of cash assets that makes an applicant, under section 96(1)﻿(b) of the Act, ineligible for temporary additional support; and
prescribes or sets out other eligibility criteria for receiving that support, the amount of it, and the period or periods for which it may be granted.

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires,—
ACC earner levies means levies payable under section 219(1) of the Accident Compensation Act 2001
allowable costs, in relation to an applicant, and subject to section 186 of the Act (benefit may be subject to repayment of insurance payment), has the meaning given to it by regulation 71
beneficiary means a person who is being paid a specified benefit
cash assets has the meaning given to it by regulation 60
chargeable income has the meaning given to it by regulation 69
consumer credit contract has the meaning given to it by section 11 of the Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act 2003
deficiency has the meaning given to it by regulation 62(2)

dependent child, in relation to a person, does not include a child in respect of whom an orphan’s benefit or an unsupported child’s benefit is being paid, but otherwise has the meaning given to it by Schedule 2 of the Act
disability costs—
means disability-related expenses, being expenses of a kind for which a disability allowance under section 85 of the Act would be payable; but

despite paragraph (a), does not include any costs for counselling (including the costs of transport to attend counselling) in excess of the amount paid for that purpose by way of a disability allowance under section 85 of the Act
disability exception amount has the meaning given to it by regulation 64

disposable income, in relation to an applicant, means the remainder (whether a positive or a negative amount) obtained by subtracting the applicant’s allowable costs from the applicant’s chargeable income
family, in relation to a person, means that person’s spouse or partner and the dependent children of the person or the person’s spouse or partner
net rate, in relation to any benefit,—

if the benefit is New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension, means the appropriate rate set out in Schedule 1 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001 or, as the case may be, the regulations specifying rates of veteran’s pension under section 169 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014, after the deduction of standard tax; or
for any other benefit, means the appropriate rate set out in the appropriate part of Schedule 4 (or of any other provision) of the Act
non-beneficiary means a person who is not a beneficiary
qualifying temporary accommodation, in relation to a person who requires temporary accommodation until such time as they have an inhabitable home, means premises—
that are occupied by the person and by any members of their family who usually reside with the person; and

that are occupied by the person because premises owned by the person and that the person usually occupied as their home at the time of the severe weather events are uninhabitable because of those events; and
for which MSD is satisfied that temporary accommodation costs are payable by the person under an agreement for temporary accommodation, for example,—
a tenancy to which the Residential Tenancies Act 1986 applies; or
a flat- or house-sharing agreement; or
a hire or rental agreement for a cabin, mobile home, or caravan; or
a lodging or boarding arrangement; or
a commercial accommodation arrangement such as a hotel, motel, or homestay
revolving credit contract has the meaning given to it by section 5 of the Credit Contracts and Consumer Finance Act 2003
severe weather events means all or any of the following weather events:
heavy rainfall commencing on 17 August 2022 and ending on 20 August 2022 in the Nelson, Tasman, and Marlborough districts:
heavy rainfall commencing on 26 January 2023 and ending on 3 February 2023 in the Northland, Auckland, Waikato, and Bay of Plenty regions:
Cyclone Gabrielle, which crossed the North Island during the period commencing on 12 February 2023 and ending on 16 February 2023
severe weather events-related temporary accommodation costs, of a person, means any temporary accommodation costs of the person if—
the person is eligible for assistance under the Severe Weather Events TAA Programme; and
the person is granted (or the person has applied for, and MSD has not yet declined to grant them) assistance under that programme
Severe Weather Events TAA Programme means the Temporary Accommodation Assistance (Severe Weather Events) Programme approved and established by the Minister under section 101(1) of the Act
specified benefits means the following:
a main benefit; and
New Zealand superannuation; and
a veteran’s pension
standard costs, in relation to an applicant, means the appropriate amount set out in Schedule 3
standard tax has the meaning given to it by section 15 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001 or by section 170 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014, whichever is applicable
temporary accommodation costs, in relation to a person,—
means the following repeated and regular, or lump sum, costs of the person payable for the provision of qualifying temporary accommodation:
for premises subject to a tenancy, flat-sharing, or house-sharing agreement, the amount payable by the person for rent of the premises:
for a cabin, mobile home, or caravan subject to a hire or rental agreement, the amount payable by the person to hire or rent the cabin, mobile home, or caravan:
for premises subject to a private lodging or boarding arrangement, the amount payable by the person for board and lodging:
for premises subject to a commercial accommodation arrangement, the amount payable by the person for that accommodation:
for premises subject to any other arrangement, an amount payable by the person in exchange for the right to occupy the premises; but
excludes the following costs, unless MSD is satisfied that they cannot be separated from the amount that is payable for the provision of the qualifying temporary accommodation:
any arrears:
any general living expenses, for example, food, electricity, gas, laundry, cleaning, parking, phone, Internet, and insurance:
costs related to the securing of the temporary accommodation, for example, any deposit, booking fees, bond, insurance, or delivery fees
temporary additional support means support of that kind under section 96 of the Act
upper limit has the meaning given to it in regulation 62(3) or 63(2), whichever is applicable
weekly qualifying accommodation costs has the meaning given to it by clause 7 of Part 7 of Schedule 4 of the Act.
