subpart.crosshead: Eligibility for temporary additional support

prov.heading: Who is eligible for support

para.text: A person may apply for temporary additional support—
whether or not the person is receiving any other benefit, pension, or allowance under the Act, the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001, or the Veterans’ Support Act 2014; but
only if the person is not, under regulation 59, ineligible for that support.

However, if a person (A) is, under regulation 59(2), ineligible for temporary additional support because a member of A’s family (B) has been granted it, temporary additional support granted to B may be reviewed to ensure that B’s allowable costs and chargeable income include A’s allowable costs and (if applicable) chargeable income.

prov.heading: Who is ineligible for support

para.text: A child who is a dependent child or a child in respect of whom an orphan’s benefit or an unsupported child’s benefit is being paid is ineligible for temporary additional support; but neither of the following is ineligible to be granted temporary additional support in respect of the child:
the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver; and
a spouse or partner of the child’s principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver.
A person is ineligible for temporary additional support if a member of the person’s family has applied for and been granted temporary additional support.
All of the following are also ineligible for temporary additional support:
a person who is 15 years old or younger:
a person to whom section 230(1) of the Act applies:

a person to whom a special benefit continues to be payable under section 23 of the Social Security (Working for Families) Amendment Act 2004 (as saved by clause 19 of Schedule 1 of the Act) and that person’s spouse or partner:
a person who is a full-time student unless—
the person is a beneficiary; or
the person, or the person’s spouse or partner, is the principal caregiver or temporary OB or UCB caregiver of 1 or more dependent children; or
the person, or a member of the person’s family, has disability costs (as defined in regulation 57)—
in respect of which a disability allowance under section 85 of the Act has been granted; but
that are in excess of the maximum amount of a disability allowance under section 85 of the Act:
a person whose cash assets together with the cash assets of the person’s spouse or partner (if any) exceed in value,—
for a single person, the amount in clause 1 of Schedule 5 of the Act; or
for a person who is in a relationship, or a sole parent, the amount in clause 2 of Schedule 5 of the Act.

history.history-note: Regulation 59(1)﻿(a): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 59(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).
Regulation 59(3)﻿(d)﻿(ii): amended, on 1 July 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Financial Assistance for Caregivers) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 25).

prov.heading: Cash assets defined

para.text: For the purposes of this subpart and section 96(1)﻿(b) of the Act, and the interpretation of cash assets in section 98 of the Act, cash assets, in relation to a person, means—
the person’s cash; and
the other assets of the person that can be converted readily into cash.
In particular, cash assets includes all of the following:
shares, stocks, debentures, bonus bonds, and other bonds; and
bank accounts, including fixed and term deposits with any bank, friendly society, credit union, or building society; and
money invested with or lent to any bank or other financial institution or other person, whether on mortgage or other security or unsecured; and
the net equity held in property or land not used as the person’s home; and
building society shares; and
bills of exchange or promissory notes; and
the person’s share in any partnership.
However, cash assets does not include any of the following:
if the person is single, the motor vehicle or other vehicle required by the person for the person’s personal use; or
if the person is in a relationship, the motor vehicles or other vehicles (not exceeding 2 in total) required by the person and the person’s spouse or partner for their personal use; or
a caravan, boat, or other vehicle—
in which the net equity is less than $2,000; or
that is used by the person or a member of the person’s family for day-to-day accommodation; or
the personal effects of the person; or
any specified item, amount, or kind of cash assets exempted from the definition in this regulation by clause 2 of Schedule 8.
Subclause (2) does not limit the generality of subclause (1); but subclause (3) overrides subclauses (1) and (2).

subpart.crosshead: Application form for, and amount, period, and form of, support

prov.heading: Application form must be completed

para.text: Temporary additional support may be granted to an applicant for that support only if an application form for the purpose is completed in writing by or on behalf of the applicant.
The form must be provided by MSD (see regulations 166 and 172).
The form must, when completed (see regulation 172(e)), set out the information required to allow—
the applicant’s eligibility for temporary additional support to be assessed; and
the rate of any temporary additional support payable to the applicant to be determined.

prov.heading: Prescribed amount of support: general weekly rate

para.text: The amount of temporary additional support that may be granted per week is the sum of—
the lesser of the deficiency and the upper limit (see subclause (3) or regulation 63, whichever is applicable); and
the disability exception amount (if that amount is available to the applicant under regulation 64).
The deficiency is the amount (if any) by which the applicant’s standard costs exceed the applicant’s disposable income (as those terms are defined in regulation 57), but—
is nil if the applicant’s disposable income exceeds the applicant’s standard costs; and
if the applicant’s disposable income is a negative amount, is the sum of that income (expressed as a positive amount) and the applicant’s standard costs.
The upper limit is 30% of the total before abatement on account of income or other deduction of the net rate per week—
of the 1 or more specified benefits payable to the applicant and the applicant’s spouse or partner (if any); or

if the applicant and the applicant’s spouse or partner (if any) are non-beneficiaries, of the benefit that would be payable to the applicant or them if jobseeker support were payable to the applicant or them.

prov.heading: Variation of upper limit for persons awaiting decision on refugee or protected person claims, refugees and protected persons, and certain applicants for residence class visas

para.text: Subclause (2) applies, instead of regulation 62(3), when calculating under regulation 62(1) the amount of temporary additional support that may be granted per week to a person who MSD is satisfied is—

a person of the kind referred to in section 205(1)﻿(a) of the Act (a person lawfully present in New Zealand awaiting the outcome of the person’s claim for recognition as a refugee or a protected person in New Zealand); or
a person referred to in section 205(1)﻿(b) of the Act (a person who is recognised as a refugee or a protected person in New Zealand); or

a person referred to in section 205(1)﻿(c) of the Act (a person applying for a residence class visa under the Immigration Act 2009 who is compelled to remain in New Zealand through unforeseen circumstances).
The upper limit is the higher of—
the appropriate maximum rate of accommodation supplement that would be applicable if section 19(1) of the Act did not prohibit the person receiving a benefit; and
the upper limit in regulation 62(3).

prov.heading: Disability exception amount

para.text: The disability exception amount is available to the applicant only if—
the deficiency exceeds the upper limit; and
the applicant’s allowable costs include an excess disability costs total.
The disability exception amount is,—
if the applicable excess costs are $100 or less, the amount of the applicable excess costs; or
if the applicable excess costs are greater than $100, $100 plus 30% of the balance of the applicable excess costs greater than $100.
In this subpart,—
applicable excess costs are the lesser of—
the deficiency minus the upper limit:
the excess disability costs total
excess disability costs, in respect of a person, means disability costs (as defined in regulation 57) that—
are disability costs in respect of which the person has been granted a disability allowance under section 85 of the Act; and
have been included in the calculation of that disability allowance granted to the person; and
are in excess of the maximum amount of a disability allowance under section 85 of the Act
excess disability costs total means the total of all of the excess disability costs (if any) for any or all of—
the applicant; and
a member of the applicant’s family; and
a child for whom an orphan’s benefit or an unsupported child’s benefit is being paid.

history.history-note: Regulation 64(1): replaced, on 1 July 2021, by regulation 5(1) of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2021 (LI 2021/121).
Regulation 64(1A): inserted, on 1 July 2021, by regulation 5(1) of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2021 (LI 2021/121).
Regulation 64(2) applicable excess costs: inserted, on 1 July 2021, by regulation 5(2) of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2021 (LI 2021/121).

prov.heading: Amount of support granted must be recalculated after change in chargeable income or allowable costs, or both

para.text: If an applicant’s chargeable income changes, the applicant’s allowable costs change, or both of them change, during the period (see regulation 67) for which the applicant has been granted temporary additional support, then despite regulation 62(1)—

the amount of that support granted to the applicant (whether it results from the original calculation under regulation 62(1) or from an earlier recalculation required by this paragraph) must, as soon as practicable, be recalculated under regulation 62(1) taking into account all changes—
in the applicant’s chargeable income or allowable costs, or both, during that period; and
that were not taken into account in the original calculation under regulation 62(1) or in any earlier recalculations required by this paragraph; and

the amount of that support granted to the applicant for any later weeks of that period (until and subject to any later recalculation required by paragraph (a)) is the amount that results from the recalculation required by paragraph (a).

prov.heading: Prescribed amount of support: exceptions

para.text: The amount of temporary additional support that may be granted per week—

is 50% of the weekly rate under regulation 62(1) if temporary additional support is granted to the spouse or partner of a person who is ineligible to be granted temporary additional support under regulation 59(3)﻿(b) or (d); and
is nil if the result of the calculation under regulation 62(1) or, as the case may be, paragraph (a), is less than $1 a week.
Subclause (1)﻿(b) overrides subclause (1)﻿(a).
This regulation overrides regulations 62 to 65.

example.heading: Example 1—single jobseeker support beneficiary with 2 dependent children

para.text: P is a single jobseeker support beneficiary with 2 dependent children, both of whom are younger than 13 years of age.

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns. It is inside an example box.

row.entry: P’s weekly chargeable income is $663.56, and is made up of—
net jobseeker support; and
family tax credit—a credit of tax under subparts MA to MG and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007; and
accommodation supplement for a resident renting in area 1; and
disability allowance (paid at the maximum).
P’s weekly allowable costs are $308.87, and are made up of—
rent in area 1 (deduct $26.05 loading from weekly qualifying accommodation costs); and
disability costs via disability allowance.
P’s disposable income is therefore $354.69, which is the remainder (a positive amount) obtained by subtracting—
P’s weekly allowable costs; from
P’s weekly chargeable income.
P’s standard costs are $376.84, and are made up of—
70% of the net jobseeker support; and
70% of family tax credit.
P’s standard costs, $376.84, exceed P’s disposable income, $354.69, by $22.15. The deficiency is therefore $22.15.
The upper limit is $100.22 (which is 30% of the net jobseeker support).

para.text: If P is eligible for temporary additional support under regulations 58 to 60, and an application form for that support is completed as required by regulation 61, then the amount of that support that P may be granted per week, under regulation 62, is $22.15, which is the lesser of the deficiency, $22.15, and the upper limit, $100.22. (Although P has disability costs included in P’s allowable costs, the disability exception amount is not available to P under regulation 64, because P’s deficiency does not exceed the upper limit.)
Note: This example is based on rates of benefits and WFF tax credits as at 26 November 2018. When using this example, readers should check the most recent rates of benefits and WFF tax credits.

example.heading: Example 2—married jobseeker support beneficiary with 3 dependent children

para.text: S is a married jobseeker support beneficiary with 3 dependent children, all of whom are younger than 13 years of age.

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns. It is inside an example box.

row.entry: S’s weekly chargeable income is $806.48, and is made up of—
net jobseeker support; and
family tax credit—a credit of tax under subparts MA to MG and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007; and
disability allowance × 2 (paid at the maximum).
S’s weekly allowable costs are $459.05, and are made up of—
Housing New Zealand rent in area 1 (deduct $26.05 loading from weekly qualifying accommodation costs); and
disability costs via disability allowance.
S’s disposable income is therefore $347.43, which is the remainder (a positive amount) obtained by subtracting—
S’s weekly allowable costs; from
S’s weekly chargeable income.
S’s standard costs are $476.03, and are made up of—
70% of the net jobseeker support; and
70% of family tax credit.
S’s standard costs, $476.03, exceed S’s disposable income, $347.43, by $128.60. The deficiency is therefore $128.60.
The upper limit is $115.35 (which is 30% of the net jobseeker support).
The applicable excess costs are $13.25, which are the lesser of—
the deficiency minus the upper limit ($128.60–$115.35); and
the excess disability costs total
The disability exception amount is $13.25 (which is the full amount of the applicable excess costs, as it is less than $100).

para.text: If S is eligible for temporary additional support under regulations 58 to 60, and an application form for that support is completed as required by regulation 61, then the amount of that support that S may be granted per week, under regulations 62 and 64, is $128.60.
That amount, $128.60, is the sum of—
$115.35, which is the lesser of the deficiency, $128.60, and the upper limit, $115.35 (see regulation 62(1)﻿(a)); and
$13.25, which is the disability exception amount available to S under regulation 64 (see regulation 62(1)﻿(b)).
Note: This example is based on rates of benefits and WFF tax credits as at 26 November 2018. When using this example, readers should check the most recent rates of benefits and WFF tax credits.

example.heading: Example 3—single jobseeker support beneficiary with no dependent children

para.text: X is a single jobseeker support beneficiary who is 26 years of age and has no dependent children.

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns. It is inside an example box

row.entry: X’s weekly chargeable income is $238.96, and is made up of—
net jobseeker support; and
disability allowance.
X’s weekly allowable costs are $158.01, and are made up of—
Housing New Zealand rent in area 1 (deduct $26.05 loading from weekly qualifying accommodation costs); and
disability costs via disability allowance; and
consumer credit contracts.
X’s disposable income is therefore $80.95, which is the remainder (a positive amount) obtained by subtracting—
X’s weekly allowable costs; from
X’s weekly chargeable income.
X’s standard costs are $150.74 (which is 70% of the net jobseeker support).
X’s standard costs, $150.74, exceed X’s disposable income, $80.95, by $69.79. The deficiency is therefore $69.79.
The upper limit is $64.60 (which is 30% of the net jobseeker support).

para.text: If X is eligible for temporary additional support under regulations 58 to 60, and an application form for that support is completed as required by regulation 61, then the amount of that support that X may be granted per week, under regulation 62, is $64.60, which is the lesser of the deficiency, $69.79, and the upper limit, $64.60. (Although X has disability costs included in X’s allowable costs, the disability exception amount is not available to X under regulation 64, because X’s disability costs are met by X’s disability allowance.)
Note: This example is based on rates of benefits and WFF tax credits as at 26 November 2018. When using this example, readers should check the most recent rates of benefits and WFF tax credits.

prov.heading: Prescribed period of support

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Prescribed period

para.text: The prescribed period referred to in section 96(3) of the Act is—
the exceptional circumstances longer fixed period (as defined by subclause (1B)), if subclause (1A) applies to the applicant; and
the shorter fixed period (as defined by subclause (3)), if subclause (2) applies to the applicant; and
13 weeks after temporary additional support commences (the standard period), in all other cases.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Exceptional circumstances longer fixed period

para.text: This subclause applies to the applicant if, when temporary additional support is granted, MSD considers that, because of exceptional circumstances, on a date (the non-qualification date) after (rather than before, or at) the end of the standard period—
the deficiency will reduce to nil; or
the applicant will for any reason be ineligible for temporary additional support; or
temporary additional support will for any reason not be payable to the applicant.
The exceptional circumstances longer fixed period means a period that is fixed by MSD, but that must—
start at the same time as, but be longer than, the standard period; and
end on or after the close of the day before the non-qualification date.
In this regulation, exceptional circumstances includes, without limitation, any of the following:
a state of local emergency or state of national emergency in force under Part 4 of the Civil Defence Emergency Management Act 2002:
effects of an outbreak of a quarantinable disease in respect of which an epidemic notice has been given, and is in force, under section 5(1) of the Epidemic Preparedness Act 2006.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Shorter fixed period

para.text: This subclause applies to the applicant if, when temporary additional support is granted, MSD considers that, on a date (the non-qualification date) before the end of the standard period,—
the deficiency will reduce to nil; or
the applicant will for any reason be ineligible for temporary additional support; or
temporary additional support will for any reason not be payable to the applicant.
The shorter fixed period means a period that is fixed by MSD, but that must—
start at the same time as, but be shorter than, the standard period; and
end on or after the close of the day before the non-qualification date.

history.history-note: Regulation 67(1) heading: inserted, on 30 March 2020, by regulation 4(1) of the Social Security (COVID-19—Temporary Additional Support and Expiry and Regrant of Benefits) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/53).

Regulation 67(2) heading: inserted, on 30 March 2020, by regulation 4(3) of the Social Security (COVID-19—Temporary Additional Support and Expiry and Regrant of Benefits) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/53).

prov.heading: No lump sums payable

para.text: No payment of temporary additional support may be made by way of a lump sum payment.
Subclause (1) does not apply to any lump sum payment of arrears of temporary additional support made to a person on or after 18 August 2017.
For the purpose of this subclause, subclause (2), and the tables in Parts 2 and 3 of Schedule 8,—
lump sum eligibility requirements, for a person (A), means that,—
on 1 April 2006, A was entitled to continue receiving the special benefit; and

during any period on or after 1 April 2006, A would have received more assistance from temporary additional support than the special benefit but was not advised by the department (as defined in section 3(1) of the Social Security Act 1964) or MSD that A could apply for temporary additional support (lump sum eligibility period); and

in response to advice by the department (as so defined) or MSD on or after 18 August 2017, A cancels the special benefit and applies for temporary additional support in respect of any lump sum eligibility period
lump sum payment of arrears of temporary additional support means a lump sum payment of arrears of temporary additional support—
made as a result of an application of section 317 of the Act; and
made to—
a person (A) who meets the lump sum eligibility requirements; or
a person (B) who, during any lump sum eligibility period, is or was the spouse or partner of A and is or was entitled, under regulation 191, to an apportionment of any other benefit payable to A; and
calculated after the deduction of the amount of the special benefit that was paid to A or B during any lump sum eligibility period
special benefit means the special benefit as continued under section 23 of the Social Security (Working for Families) Amendment Act 2004 (as saved by clause 19 of Schedule 1 of the Act).

subpart.crosshead: Chargeable income

prov.heading: Chargeable income

para.text: For the purposes of this subpart and of section 98 of the Act, chargeable income, in relation to a person, includes the weekly total of all of the items in subclause (3), after deducting the amount (if any) MSD estimates or determines is payable (where applicable) for income tax, for ACC earner levies, or for both, on any of those items.
A reference in this subpart to a person includes a reference to the person’s spouse or partner.
The items referred to in subclause (1) are (subject to regulation 70)—
the net rate of any benefit payable to the person under the Act (other than an orphan’s benefit, an unsupported child’s benefit, a winter energy payment, or any childcare assistance):
the amount of any credit of tax under subparts MA to MG and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007 that is—
paid to the person by instalments in accordance with sections 80KI to 80KU of the Tax Administration Act 1994; or
not paid by instalments but is one to which, in MSD’s opinion, the person is entitled under that subpart:
any parental leave payment payable to the person under Part 7A of the Parental Leave and Employment Protection Act 1987:
any pension or allowance payable under the Veterans’ Support Act 2014:

the rate of any New Zealand superannuation payable to the person under the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001 or any veteran’s pension payable to the person under the Veterans’ Support Act 2014:

income as defined in Part 2 of Schedule 3 of the Act (including, without limitation, a payment that is weekly income under clauses 3 and 8 and Part 3A of Schedule 3 of the Act because it is an information share child support payment):
a child support excess payment, which includes, without limitation, a mixed child support payment (see clause 8(na) of Schedule 3 of the Act):
the amount of any periodical payments (for example, those included by subclause (4)﻿(a))—
of special assistance payable to the person under section 101(1) of the Act under any welfare programme; and

that, in the opinion of MSD, are for the purpose of replacing lost income or maintaining or supplementing the person’s income and not for the purposes of paying or reimbursing specified costs of the person:
the amount of any accommodation benefit, basic grant, or independent circumstances grant payable to the person under the Student Allowances Regulations 1998:
the living-costs component of any student loan payable to the person.
Payments under subclause (3)﻿(g)—

include, without limitation, any payments payable to the person under the Telephone Costs Payment Programme established and approved by the Minister under section 124(1)﻿(d) of the Social Security Act 1964 (and continued under clause 21 of Schedule 1 of the Act); but
do not include any payments for childcare assistance or any payments made under any of the programmes specified in subclause (5).
The programmes referred to in subclause (4)﻿(b) (and approved and established under section 101(1) of the Act or, as the case requires, continued under clause 21 of Schedule 1 of the Act) are—
the Away from Home Allowance Programme:
the Home Help Programme:
the Social Rehabilitation Assistance Programme:
the Special Transfer Allowance Programme 2000:
the Families Package (Transitional Assistance) Programme:
the Employment and Work Readiness Assistance Programme:
the Income Support Package (Transitional Assistance) Programme:
the Abatement Threshold Increase (Transitional Assistance) Programme 2021:
the Benefit Rate Increase (Transitional Assistance) Programme 2021:
the Severe Weather Events TAA Programme.

In calculating the amount of a person’s chargeable income, MSD must disregard any deduction or reduction of the amount of any item of that income made or imposed by or under any statutory power, or under any deduction notice or attachment order or otherwise, on account of any debt, liability, sanction, penalty, child support payment, or student loan repayment.
Subclause (6) overrides subclauses (1) to (5).

history.history-note: Regulation 69(3)﻿(f): replaced, on 1 July 2023, by section 52(1) of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).
Regulation 69(3)﻿(fa): inserted, on 1 July 2023, by section 52(1) of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).

Regulation 69(5)﻿(g): amended, on 17 November 2025, by regulation 5 of the Social Security (Youth Money Management) Amendment Regulations 2025 (SL 2025/223).
Regulation 69(5)﻿(h): inserted, on 1 April 2020, by regulation 5 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/15).
Regulation 69(5)﻿(i): inserted, on 1 April 2021, by regulation 5 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations 2021 (LI 2021/17).
Regulation 69(5)﻿(j): inserted, on 1 July 2021, by regulation 7 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2021 (LI 2021/121).

prov.heading: Further limits on chargeable income: exempted payments and income

para.text: The items referred to in regulation 69 do not include payments and income exempted by Parts 2 and 3 of Schedule 8.

prov.heading: Allowable costs

para.text: For the purposes of this subpart and of section 98 of the Act, allowable costs—
means the regular essential expenses (as defined in subclause (2)) (if any) of the applicant and the applicant’s family reckoned on a weekly basis; and therefore
does not include an expense that is not an essential expense (as so defined).

In subclause (1), essential expense means an expense of a kind, and within the relevant limits (if any), specified in subclause (3), and that (unless the expense is one referred to in subclause (3)﻿(b), (c), (e)﻿(i), (h), or (i) and is in respect of an item acquired in accordance with subclause (4)), in MSD’s opinion,—
is essential for a person to pay or incur in order to meet the daily living needs of the person, of members of the person’s family, or of both; and
could not, when the expense or the liability for the expense was incurred, readily be avoided or varied.
The kinds of expenses and limits referred to in subclause (2) are as follows:
the person’s weekly qualifying accommodation costs (as defined in subclause (5)) less $34.13:

agreed period payments (as defined in subclause (6)), not exceeding the maximum weekly amount in column 3 of the following table, and made in connection with the acquisition of any of the items in column 1 of that table (within the maximum number of those items in column 2 of that table):

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and contains 3 columns. Column 1 is headed Essential household item. Column 2 is headed Maximum number of items. Column 3 is headed Maximum weekly amount ($).

row.entry: Beds (including mattresses)
Combined refrigerator-freezer
Separate refrigerator and separate freezer
1 of each if separate items
31.80 in total if separate items
Dining suite
Lounge suite
Portable heaters
Washing machine
Stove
Television set

para.text: revolving credit payments (as defined in subclause (7)) for any of the items in column 1 of the table in paragraph (b) (being items acquired in accordance with subclause (4) and within the maximum numbers in column 2 of that table) and not exceeding (in terms of the rate of the payments, and either on a monthly or a weekly basis) the lesser of the maximum monthly payment (as defined in subclause (8)) and the maximum weekly payment in column 3 of that table:

costs for laundry or laundrette services, being costs incurred instead of regular payments for a washing machine or within a period during which the applicant could reasonably arrange to have repaired a washing machine available to the applicant, and not exceeding $31.80 per week:
disability costs (as defined in regulation 57),—
including any disability costs that are payments to which subclause (9) or (10) applies; but
excluding in all cases any costs of residential care services in respect of which temporary additional support must not be granted under section 96(2) of the Act:

for a person who is eligible for assistance under the Telephone Costs Payment Programme approved by the Minister under section 124(1)﻿(d) of the Social Security Act 1964 (and continued under clause 21 of Schedule 1 of the Act), and if paragraph (m) of this subclause does not apply, the weekly amount of telephone rental costs (as defined in subclause (11)) that were, immediately before 1 April 1999,—
claimed by the person; and
treated by MSD as an additional expense under section 69C(2A)﻿(a) of the Social Security Act 1964:

running costs, at a per kilometre rate set by MSD, for a motor vehicle where no suitable public transport is available for the purpose of transporting the applicant and the applicant’s spouse or partner to and from their places of employment and where the vehicle is essential for that purpose:
agreed period payments (as defined in subclause (6)) not exceeding $85.96 a week made in connection with the acquisition of a motor vehicle or other vehicle (for example, a bicycle)—

for the applicant and the applicant’s spouse or partner (if any) (whether or not either or both of them are beneficiaries), where no suitable public transport is available for the relevant purpose and the vehicle is essential—
for the purpose of transporting the applicant and the applicant’s spouse or partner to and from their places of employment; or
for the purpose of transporting the applicant or a member of the applicant’s family who is chronically ill or has a disability; or
for an applicant who is a beneficiary where—
there is no public transport reasonably available to the applicant; and
the contract or arrangement to acquire the vehicle was entered into before the applicant became a beneficiary:

revolving credit payments (as defined in subclause (7)) for a motor vehicle or other vehicle (for example, a bicycle) acquired in accordance with subclause (4) and where paragraph (h)﻿(i) or (ii)﻿(A) applies to the applicant and vehicle, and not exceeding (in terms of the rate of the payments, and either on a monthly or a weekly basis) the lesser of the maximum monthly payment (as defined in subclause (8)) and $85.96 per week:
the costs of public transport of the applicant and the applicant’s spouse or partner to and from their places of employment:

the net costs of essential childcare (that is, the gross costs of essential childcare, as defined in subclause (12), minus any childcare assistance payable under section 77 of the Act or by way of special assistance under section 101 of the Act):

essential expenses in respect of a child in the care of the applicant or the applicant’s spouse or partner for whom an orphan’s benefit or an unsupported child’s benefit is paid that are not able to be met from the total of that benefit and any disability allowance payable in respect of the child:
telephone rental costs (as defined in subclause (11)) for an applicant for whom, in the opinion of MSD, a telephone is a necessity—
because of the applicant’s special family circumstances (for example, to enable an applicant living in a rural location to be contacted by the school that the applicant’s children attend); or
to help to ensure the applicant’s personal safety or security (for example, a frail person living alone, or a separated person with a protection order against a spouse or partner); or
because of the applicant’s employment conditions (for example, an electrical worker on call 24 hours per day):

any child support required to be paid by a person for the week concerned under a formula assessment under the Child Support Act 1991 (regardless of whether, and, if so, how that support must under that Act be paid to, or otherwise dealt with to improve the position of, a receiving carer).
An item is acquired in accordance with this subclause for the purposes of subclauses (2), (3)﻿(c) and (i), and (10) if,—

when the item was acquired, neither the applicant nor the applicant’s spouse or partner (if any) was a beneficiary (whether or not either of them was a beneficiary, or both of them were beneficiaries, at any time before the item was acquired); and

at the time of the application for temporary additional support, the applicant or the applicant’s spouse or partner (if any) is a beneficiary, or both of them are beneficiaries, or neither of them is a beneficiary at that time but one or both of them became a beneficiary after the item was acquired.
For the purposes of subclause (3)﻿(a), a person’s weekly qualifying accommodation costs are that person’s weekly qualifying accommodation costs as defined in regulation 57 but—
do not include the following costs:
any costs of residential care services in respect of which temporary additional support must not be granted under section 96(2) of the Act; and

any severe weather events-related temporary accommodation costs of the person (even if those costs exceed the rate of, or are paid or incurred after the end of, or are otherwise not met or not to be met by, assistance under the Severe Weather Events TAA Programme that has been or may be granted to the person); and
include arrears except any that were incurred while the person or the person’s spouse or partner was in receipt of—
an accommodation benefit under the Student Allowances Regulations 1998; or
an accommodation supplement, a rent rebate allowance, or a tenure protection allowance under the Act; or
an income-related rent (as defined in section 2(1) of the Public and Community Housing Management Act 1992); or
assistance under the Severe Weather Events TAA Programme; and
include an emergency housing contribution (as that term is defined in regulation 3(1)); and
include a transitional housing contribution (as that term is defined in regulation 3(1)).

Agreed period payments, in relation to an item referred to in any of subclauses (3)﻿(b) and (h) and (9), means payments, during the agreed period, under a consumer credit contract or other arrangement that—
is evidenced in writing to the satisfaction of MSD; and
requires regular payments over an agreed period; and
was entered into to acquire the item or to repay debt incurred in acquiring the item; and

is not a revolving credit contract, an arrangement relating to an advance payment of all or part of 1 or more instalments of a benefit under section 347 of the Act, or an arrangement relating to a recoverable grant of assistance under section 101 of the Act.
Revolving credit payments, in relation to an item referred to in any of subclauses (3)﻿(c) and (i) and (10), means payments—
under a revolving credit contract (for example, a credit card contract) that requires regular payments and that was used to acquire the item; and
not exceeding (in total) the lesser of the cash price of the item and the amount owing under the contract at the time of the application for temporary additional support; and

for the period of 20 months after the acquisition of the item, if the amount owing under the contract at the time of the application for temporary additional support is equal to or greater than the cash price of the item; and
for whichever of the following periods ends first, if the amount owing under the contract at the time of the application for temporary additional support is less than the cash price of the item:
the period of 20 months after the acquisition of the item; and
the period in whole months that MSD estimates is necessary for that amount owing to be repaid fully at a rate of payment that is the maximum monthly payment (as defined in subclause (8)).
The maximum monthly payment, in relation to an item to which subclause (3)﻿(c) or (i) or (10) applies, means 5% of the cash price of the item.

The payments (referred to in subclause (3)﻿(e)﻿(i)) to which this subclause applies are agreed period payments (as defined in subclause (6)) not exceeding $31.80 per week made in connection with the acquisition of 1 clothes dryer for a person for whom the costs of acquiring a clothes dryer are additional costs arising from the person’s disability.

The payments (referred to in subclause (3)﻿(e)﻿(i)) to which this subclause applies are revolving credit payments (as defined in subclause (7)) for 1 clothes dryer, acquired in accordance with subclause (4), for a person for whom the costs of acquiring a clothes dryer are additional costs arising from the person’s disability, and not exceeding (in terms of the rate of the payments, and either on a monthly or a weekly basis) the lesser of the maximum monthly payment (as defined in subclause (8)) and $31.80 per week.
In subclause (3)﻿(f) and (m), telephone rental costs—
means the regular and ongoing costs of renting a basic telephone, or of telephone line rental, or of both, as well as any wiring maintenance charges paid; and therefore
does not include toll charges.
In subclause (3)﻿(k), gross costs of essential childcare, in relation to an applicant for temporary additional support, means the costs, up to a maximum of $6 per child per hour, of childcare—
that enables the applicant, or the applicant’s spouse or partner, or both, to take part in employment; or
that is required because the applicant is, or the applicant’s spouse or partner is, or both of them are, seriously disabled or seriously ill.

part.heading: Obligations

prov.heading: Guide to Part 3

para.text: Part 3 deals with the following topics:
subpart 1 (regulation 73) deals with when work-test obligations apply:
subpart 2 (regulations 75 to 80) deals with deferral of work-test obligations:
subpart 3 (regulations 81 to 89) provides for drug testing of beneficiaries:
subpart 4 (regulations 90 to 92) deals with social obligations in relation to dependent children:
subpart 5 (regulation 93) prescribes health practitioners for social obligations of a young person granted a young parent payment and relating to health care enrolment of a dependent child:
subpart 6 (regulation 94) prescribes health practitioners for obligations of a young person who is a spouse or partner of a beneficiary and relating to health care enrolment of a dependent child:
subpart 7 (regulations 95 and 96) provides for participation allowances:
subpart 8 (regulations 97 to 104) provides for exemptions from work-preparation or work-test obligations:
subpart 9 (regulations 105 to 107) sets out various exemptions from obligations under the Act of certain young people.

history.history-note: Regulation 72(b): amended, on 8 November 2021, by section 12 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: Application of work-test obligations

para.text: This regulation prescribes days of the week for the purposes of, or under, sections 143(1)﻿(b) and 418(1)﻿(e) of the Act.
The work-test obligations set out in section 144(b) and (c) of the Act apply Monday to Sunday (inclusive).
The work-test obligation set out in section 146(1)﻿(d) of the Act applies Monday to Sunday (inclusive).

prov.heading: Deferral of work-test obligations of person with dependent child under age of 1 year

history.history-note: Regulation 74: revoked, on 8 November 2021, by section 13 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: Mandatory deferral of work-test obligations if person determined under section 141(1) of Act to lack capacity for part-time work

para.text: This regulation applies to a person who—
receives jobseeker support on the ground of health condition, injury, or disability; and
is the subject of a determination under section 141(1) of the Act that the person does not have the capacity for part-time work.

MSD must, after determining under section 141(1) of the Act that the person does not have capacity for part-time work (and whether or not the person has applied to defer that person’s work-test obligations), defer that person’s work-test obligations.

prov.heading: Deferral of work-test obligations on ground of later health condition, injury, or disability

para.text: This regulation applies to a person who MSD is satisfied—
receives jobseeker support; and
is subject to work-test obligations; and
has a health condition, an injury, or a disability that first arose or became apparent after the grant of jobseeker support; and
because of the health condition, injury, or disability, has no capacity for work or capacity only for work that is less than part-time work.
On the application of a person to whom this regulation applies or of its own initiative, MSD may defer all of that person’s work-test obligations.

prov.heading: Deferral of work-test obligations for young person required to receive youth services and on ground of undertaking study

para.text: This regulation applies to a person who is receiving jobseeker support if MSD is satisfied that—
the person is a young person who is required to receive youth services under section 165(2) of the Act; and
the person is subject to work-test obligations; and

the person’s undertaking of a full-time course that will give the person credits towards NCEA level 2 or an equivalent qualification would reduce the person’s risk of long-term welfare dependency (as defined in section 165(9) of the Act); and
the deferral of all of the person’s work-test obligations is necessary to enable the person to undertake that study.
On the application of a person to whom this regulation applies or of its own initiative, MSD may defer all of the person’s work-test obligations.

prov.heading: Deferral of work-test obligations for person receiving youth payment and on ground of undertaking study

para.text: This regulation applies to a person who is an applicant for jobseeker support if MSD is satisfied that—
the person has become eligible for jobseeker support while receiving a youth payment; and
the person is subject to work-test obligations; and

the person is a student in a full-time course of secondary instruction or tertiary education that will give the person credits towards NCEA level 2 or an equivalent or higher qualification that the person commenced or was undertaking while receiving the youth payment; and
the deferral of all of the person’s work-test obligations is necessary to enable the person to complete study towards that qualification.
On the application of a person to whom this regulation applies or of its own initiative, MSD may defer all of that person’s work-test obligations.

prov.heading: Procedure for deferral of work-test obligations

para.text: MSD may require an applicant for deferral of the applicant’s work-test obligations to verify particulars relevant to the application in the manner that MSD specifies, either generally or specifically.
A deferral under regulation 75, 76, 77, or 78 must be in writing and may be granted—
for a period set by MSD; or
on conditions set by MSD; or
both for a period, and on conditions, set by MSD.
Before deciding whether to defer a person’s work-test obligations under regulation 75 or 76, MSD must have regard to—
the certificate for the purpose of section 27 of the Act and any relevant report obtained under section 28 of the Act; and
any relevant work ability assessment under sections 115 to 119 of the Act.

history.history-note: Regulation 79(2): amended, on 8 November 2021, by section 14(1) of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).
Regulation 79(3): amended, on 8 November 2021, by section 14(2) of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: MSD may review, and extend, vary, or revoke, deferral of work-test obligations

para.text: MSD may—
review a deferral granted under regulation 75, 76, 77, or 78; and
extend, vary, or revoke it.

history.history-note: Regulation 80(a): amended, on 8 November 2021, by section 15 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires,—
AS/NZS 4308:2008 means the Australian/New Zealand Standard AS/NZS 4308:2008—Procedures for specimen collection and the detection and quantitation of drugs of abuse in urine
sample includes a sample that is or is referred to as a specimen
suitably qualified,—

for a person and a screening drug test, means that the person is qualified to provide that test because the person complies with (and is therefore a collector under) clause 1.3.14 of AS/NZS 4308:2008; and

for a person and recognised services for drug issue assessment and assistance (as defined in regulation 85(3)), means a health practitioner, or other person, who MSD is satisfied on reasonable grounds is suitably qualified to provide those services
testing, of a sample, includes collection, storage, handling, and dispatch of the sample.

In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires, terms used and not defined in these regulations or in the Act, but defined in AS/NZS 4308:2008, have the meanings given to them by AS/NZS 4308:2008.

prov.heading: Evidential drug test: standard prescribed

para.text: This regulation prescribes a standard—
for the purposes of the definition (in section 152 of the Act) of evidential drug test; and
as contemplated by paragraph (c) of that definition.
The standard that this regulation prescribes for those purposes is the following provisions of AS/NZS 4308:2008 (if, or insofar as, the following provisions apply to evidentiary testing):
section 1:
section 2:
section 3:
section 5:
Appendices B to E.
Evidentiary testing, of a sample, means testing of the sample—
for evidential purposes (but not for screening purposes); and
that involves use of laboratory confirmatory procedures for the unequivocal identification and quantitation of controlled drugs.

prov.heading: Screening drug test: standard prescribed

para.text: This regulation prescribes a standard—
for the purposes of the definition (in section 152 of the Act) of screening drug test; and
as contemplated by paragraph (c)﻿(iii) of that definition.

The standard that this regulation prescribes for those purposes is the following provisions of AS/NZS 4308:2008 (if, or insofar as, the following provisions apply to screening testing, and subject to the modification in subclause (4)):
section 1:
section 2:
section 3:
section 4 (except the requirement in clause 4.10 that samples with results equal to, or greater than, the cut-off in Table 1 must be subjected to confirmatory testing):
Appendix A (if the screening testing is carried out not by way of laboratory screening but on site):
Appendices B to E.
Screening testing, of a sample, means testing of the sample—
for screening purposes (and evidential purposes); and
that involves use of a procedure for on-site or laboratory screening for controlled drugs; and
that does not involve laboratory confirmatory procedures for the unequivocal identification and quantitation of controlled drugs.

The modification referred to in subclause (2) is that the requirements in clauses 2.4 and 2.5, and in Appendix A, clause A3.2, paragraph (i), for dispatch of the sample to the laboratory do not apply if, after receiving from the potential employer or training provider advice that complies with subclause (5) on the effect of a waiver of those requirements, the beneficiary waives (in any manner) those requirements.
Advice from the potential employer or training provider on the effect of a waiver of those requirements must communicate that a waiver of those requirements—

results in the sample not being available for evidentiary testing (as defined in regulation 82(3)) for any purposes (for example, for the purposes of the beneficiary’s disputing under section 257 of the Act a sanction to be imposed under any of sections 234 to 238 of the Act); and
is therefore also a waiver of evidentiary testing (as so defined) for all purposes of the sample; and

results (in the absence of a good and sufficient reason described in section 250 of the Act) in a sanction under any of sections 234 to 238 of the Act being able to be imposed on the beneficiary based solely on the beneficiary’s having failed a screening drug test.

Nothing in this regulation limits or affects the right under section 149(1) of the Act of a candidate for employment or training to waive (in any manner) an evidential drug test requested by a potential employer or a training provider.

Nothing in this regulation limits or affects the right of a beneficiary to whom section 257 of the Act applies to dispute under that section a sanction to be imposed on the beneficiary (the beneficiary’s benefit being reduced, suspended, or cancelled), under any of sections 234 to 238 of the Act, based on the beneficiary’s having failed a screening drug test, by having the sample taken from the beneficiary for the screening drug test (if the beneficiary has not waived the requirement for dispatch of that sample to the laboratory) subjected to an evidential drug test.

prov.heading: Maximum reimbursement amounts prescribed

para.text: This regulation prescribes for the purposes of section 151 of the Act maximum amounts that MSD may reimburse an employer for the actual and reasonable costs of a screening drug test, an evidential drug test, or both, that the employer has (in accordance with section 147 of the Act) requested in respect of a work-tested beneficiary.
The maximum reimbursement amounts that this regulation prescribes are set out in Schedule 4.

prov.heading: Good and sufficient reason for specified failures to comply: ground specified

para.text: This regulation prescribes for the purposes of section 250(1)﻿(e) of the Act a ground on which a beneficiary may for the purposes of section 153 or 154(a) of the Act have a good and sufficient reason for either or both of the following specified failures:
not complying with a drug testing obligation under section 147 of the Act:
failing to apply for suitable employment that requires candidates to undertake drug tests.
The ground prescribed by this regulation is that—
the person has completed a screening process that has identified the person (based on information the person provided) as—
a user of (even if not dependent on, or addicted to) 1 or more controlled drugs; and
a person who requires or may require support in addressing the person’s use (including, without limitation, the causes of the person’s use) of 1 or more controlled drugs; and
the person has (in any manner) agreed to receive services for drug issue assessment and assistance for a period that—
is reasonable for that purpose; and
has been recommended by a suitably qualified provider of services of that kind; and
the period referred to in paragraph (b) has not expired.
Recognised services for drug issue assessment and assistance means services—

to assess any needs of the recipient for support in addressing the recipient’s use (including, without limitation, the causes of the recipient’s use) of 1 or more controlled drugs, give the recipient support of that kind, or both; and
that will be, or have been, provided by a suitably qualified provider of services of that kind.

prov.heading: Requirements for drug test

para.text: A compliant drug test of a person is—
a screening drug test of the person undertaken without any associated later evidential drug test of the person; or
an evidential drug test of the person if the person has failed any associated prior screening drug test under paragraph (a); or
an evidential drug test of the person undertaken without any associated prior screening drug test of the person.

prov.heading: Use of drug test result

para.text: This regulation prescribes, for the purposes of sections 150 and 431(1)﻿(b) of the Act, how MSD may use the result of a drug test undertaken under section 147 of the Act and disclosed to MSD under section 149(2) of the Act.
MSD may use the result for all or any of the following purposes:
for the purposes of all or any of sections 124 to 140, and subpart 2 of Part 5, of the Act:
for the purposes of a review or an appeal against a decision under all or any of the provisions specified in paragraph (a).
MSD must not use or communicate the result for any other purpose.

prov.heading: Costs of drug test

para.text: MSD may reimburse a potential employer for the costs of a drug test under section 147 of the Act requested by the employer if—

reimbursement of the costs is not prevented by section 151(2) of the Act (on the costs of an evidential drug test if the person waives the right to an evidential drug test in the situation specified in section 149(1) of the Act); and
the costs are the actual and reasonable costs of the test and do not exceed the prescribed maximum amount of reimbursement set out in Schedule 4 of these regulations; and
MSD has been provided with the result of the test; and
MSD is satisfied that the result relates to the person required to undergo the test; and
MSD is satisfied that the person required to undergo the test has failed the test.

prov.heading: Costs of drug test: supplementary provisions

para.text: Regulation 88 does not apply to the costs of a screening drug test if the person who undertook the test has later undertaken and passed an associated evidential drug test requested by the potential employer.
The amount that is reimbursed to a potential employer under regulation 88 is a debt due to the Crown from the person who undertook the drug test.

subpart.heading: Social obligations in relation to dependent children

prov.heading: Definitions relating to social obligations

para.text: For the purposes of sections 131 to 134 of the Act,—
approved early childhood education programme means—
an approved early childhood education programme (as defined in regulation 20 for the purposes of regulations made under section 424 of the Act); or

an early childhood education programme approved by MSD having regard to all relevant approval criteria (if any) prescribed for the purposes of this paragraph by the Minister under section 430 of the Act; or
a programme of early childhood education provided by a school that is currently designated under section 196(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020 as a distance school.
core check, for a child and a programme to which section 134(2) of the Act applies, means a check—
of the child’s health and well-being; and
done under, and identified in contract or funding arrangements as an essential part of, the programme; and
funded, directly or indirectly, through Vote Health
registered school has the meaning given by section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020.

history.history-note: Regulation 90 approved early childhood education programme paragraph (b): replaced, on 28 October 2021, by regulation 120 of the Legislation Act (Sub-delegated Secondary Legislation) Regulations 2021 (LI 2021/248).
Regulation 90 approved early childhood education programme paragraph (c): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).
Regulation 90 registered school: amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

prov.heading: Prescribed target number of hours of attendance at approved early childhood education programme

para.text: This regulation prescribes a target number of hours of attendance at every approved early childhood education programme for the purposes of sections 131(1)﻿(b) and 132(1)﻿(a) of the Act.
The target number of hours that this regulation prescribes for those purposes is 15 hours per week.

prov.heading: Health care enrolment: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is either of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 134 of the Act (which relates to ensuring that a dependent child is enrolled with a primary health care provider who is a prescribed health practitioner who is a provider of primary health care):
a medical practitioner:
a nurse practitioner.

subpart.heading: Social obligations of young person granted young parent payment: health care enrolment of dependent child

prov.heading: Health care enrolment: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is either of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 164 of the Act (which relates to ensuring that a dependent child is enrolled with a primary health care provider who is a prescribed health practitioner who is a provider of primary health care):
a medical practitioner:
a nurse practitioner.

subpart.heading: Obligations of young person who is spouse or partner of beneficiary: health care enrolment of dependent child

prov.heading: Health care enrolment: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is either of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 167 of the Act (which relates to ensuring that a dependent child is enrolled with a primary health care provider who is a prescribed health practitioner who is a provider of primary health care):
a medical practitioner:
a nurse practitioner.

prov.heading: Participation allowance

para.text: MSD may pay any beneficiary a participation allowance in respect of the beneficiary’s participation under the Act in an activity of the kind described in the first column of Schedule 5.
The relevant rates of participation allowance are set out in the second column of Schedule 5.

prov.heading: Reimbursement portion of participation allowance

para.text: If a participation allowance includes a maximum amount for reimbursement of a beneficiary’s actual costs of participation in an activity, MSD must determine the amount to be paid to the beneficiary.

subpart.heading: Exemptions from work-preparation or work-test obligations

prov.heading: Procedure for grant of exemption

para.text: MSD may grant an exemption under section 158 of the Act—
on the application of the person concerned; or
on its own initiative.
MSD may require an applicant for an exemption under section 158 of the Act to verify particulars that are relevant to the application in the manner that MSD specifies.
Under section 158(2) of the Act, MSD—
may grant the exemption for a period set by MSD; and
may make the exemption subject to conditions set by MSD.

prov.heading: MSD may review, and extend, vary, or revoke, exemption

para.text: MSD may from time to time review an exemption granted under section 158 of the Act and may extend, vary, or revoke it.

prov.heading: Definition of special needs in relation to child

para.text: In this subpart and in regulation 107, unless the context otherwise requires, special needs, in relation to a child, means that the child has 1 or more of the following:
a physical or an intellectual disability:
a medical condition:
a chronic or recurring illness:
a learning or behavioural difficulty:
a need to adjust to a new placement as a foster child.

prov.heading: Exemption from work-preparation obligations

para.text: A person to whom section 124 or 125 of the Act applies may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of the person’s obligations under section 124 or 125 of the Act, on a ground specified in subclause (2).
A person described in subclause (1) may be granted an exemption from some or all of the person’s obligations under section 124 or 125 of the Act on the grounds that—
the person has recently changed the person’s living circumstances because of family violence and needs time to adjust to the change; or
in the case of a person who falls within section 121 of the Act, the person needs time to adjust because—
the person’s spouse or partner has recently died; or
the person has recently separated from or ceased to live together with the person’s spouse or partner; or
the person’s de facto relationship has recently ended; or
the person is caring full-time for another person whose health condition, injury, or disability requires full-time care; or
the person has a health condition, an injury, or a disability that prevents the person from meeting the person’s obligations; or
the person is the principal caregiver of a dependent child or foster child with special needs (as defined in regulation 99)—
whose welfare would be unduly harmed if the person met the person’s obligations; and
for whom satisfactory alternative care arrangements are not available to, or are unable to be made by, the caregiver; or
the person’s capacity to meet the person’s obligations is limited because that person is subject to—
a sentence of supervision under the Sentencing Act 2002; or
release conditions (as that term is defined in section 4(1) of the Parole Act 2002); or
conditions of release on bail under any enactment; or
any other sentence or order imposed by a court.

prov.heading: Exemption from work-test obligations: all work-tested beneficiaries

para.text: Any beneficiary to whom the work test applies may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of the beneficiary’s work-test obligations, on any ground specified in subclause (2).
A person described in subclause (1) may be granted an exemption from the work test on the ground that—
the person has recently changed the person’s living circumstances because of family violence and needs time to adjust to the change; or
the person’s benefit is being paid under an adopted reciprocity agreement, and the person resides in a country other than New Zealand; or
the person is serving a sentence of home detention under the Sentencing Act 2002; or
the person’s capacity to meet the obligations of the work test is limited because that person is subject to—
a sentence of supervision under the Sentencing Act 2002; or
release conditions (as that term is defined in section 4(1) of the Parole Act 2002); or
conditions of release on bail under any enactment; or
any other sentence or order imposed by a court; or
the person needs time to adjust because—
the person’s spouse or partner has recently died; or
the person has recently separated from or ceased to live together with the person’s spouse or partner; or
the person’s de facto relationship has recently ended.
In determining whether to grant an exemption from some or all of a person’s work-test obligations on the ground specified in subclause (2)﻿(a), MSD may consider, among other things, whether—
the person is living in temporary accommodation (for example, a refuge) while seeking more permanent accommodation:
the exemption is required to allow the person to help any children who are in the person’s care to adjust to the new family situation.

prov.heading: Exemption from work-test obligations: work-tested spouses or partners

para.text: A work-tested spouse or partner may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of that person’s work-test obligations, on any ground specified in regulation 101 or in subclause (2).
A person described in subclause (1) may be granted an exemption from some or all of the person’s work-test obligations on the ground that—
it would be unreasonable to expect the person to undertake employment because—
the person is the principal caregiver of a dependent child or foster child with special needs (as defined in regulation 99)—
whose welfare would be unduly harmed if the person attended employment or training; and
for whom satisfactory alternative care arrangements are not available to, or are unable to be made by, the caregiver; or
the person would qualify for a supported living payment on the ground of restricted work capacity or total blindness under section 34 of the Act; or
the person is engaged in study of a kind approved by MSD and was so engaged when the person became subject to the work test; or
the person is caring on a full-time basis for a person with a health condition, an injury, or a disability of a kind that requires that person to be cared for full-time; or
the person is engaged in full-time study of a kind approved by MSD; or
both the following apply:

the person is actively engaged in teaching the person’s dependent child or foster child who is aged 5 years or is exempt from enrolment in school under an exemption granted under section 38 or 39 of the Education and Training Act 2020; and
it would be unreasonable to expect the child to attend school because of the family’s circumstances; or
the person has primary responsibility for the day-to-day care of a child, other than on a temporary basis, and the child—
is aged less than 3 years; and
is not a dependent child of that person; or
the person is—
at least 27 weeks pregnant; or
less than 27 weeks pregnant, if MSD is satisfied that the person is suffering from complications arising from the pregnancy; or
the person meets the criteria for jobseeker support on the health condition, injury, or disability ground in section 21(1)﻿(b) or 22(c) of the Act.

In determining whether to grant an exemption from some or all of a person’s work-test obligations on the ground that subclause (2)﻿(d)﻿(ii) applies to the person, MSD may consider, among other things, whether—
there are particular needs or reasons that prevent the child from attending school (for example, the child has special needs or has been excluded or expelled from school):
there are other mitigating circumstances involved (for example, home schooling is a reasonable option because the nearest school is a significant distance away).

history.history-note: Regulation 102(2)﻿(d)﻿(i): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

prov.heading: Exemption from work-test obligations: work-tested sole parents receiving jobseeker support

para.text: A work-tested beneficiary who is a sole parent in receipt of jobseeker support may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of that beneficiary’s work-test obligations on any of the following grounds:
any ground specified in regulation 101:
any ground specified in subclause (2).
A person described in subclause (1) may be granted an exemption from some or all of the person’s work-test obligations on the ground that—

it would be unreasonable to expect the person to undertake employment because the person is the principal caregiver of a dependent child or foster child with special needs (as defined in regulation 99)—
whose welfare would be unduly harmed if the person attended employment or training; and
for whom satisfactory alternative care arrangements are not available to, or unable to be made by, the caregiver; or
both of the following apply:

the person is actively engaged in teaching the person’s dependent child or foster child who is aged 5 years or is exempt from enrolment in school under an exemption granted under section 38 or 39 of the Education and Training Act 2020; and
it would be unreasonable to expect the child to attend school because of the family’s circumstances; or
the person has primary responsibility for the day-to-day care of a child, other than on a temporary basis, and the child—
is aged less than 3 years; and
is not a dependent child of that person.

In determining whether to grant an exemption from some or all of a person’s work-test obligations on the ground that subclause (2)﻿(b)﻿(ii) applies to the person, MSD may consider, among other things, whether—
there are particular needs or reasons that prevent the child from attending school (for example, the child has special needs or has been excluded or expelled from school):
there are other mitigating circumstances involved (for example, home schooling is a reasonable option because the nearest school is a significant distance away).

history.history-note: Regulation 103(2)﻿(b)﻿(i): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

prov.heading: Exemption from work-test obligations: work-tested sole parent support beneficiaries

para.text: A work-tested sole parent support beneficiary may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of that person’s work-test obligations, on any ground specified in regulation 101 or 103 or in subclause (2).
A person described in subclause (1) may be granted an exemption from some or all of that person’s work-test obligations on the ground that—

it would be unreasonable to expect the person to undertake employment because the person would qualify for a supported living payment on the ground of restricted work capacity or total blindness under section 34 of the Act; or
the person is—
at least 27 weeks pregnant; or
less than 27 weeks pregnant, if MSD is satisfied that the person is suffering from complications arising from the pregnancy; or
the person meets the criteria for jobseeker support on the health condition, injury, or disability ground in section 21(1)﻿(b) or 22(c) of the Act; or

the person is engaged in full-time study at a level higher than NCEA level 3 on the Qualifications and Credentials Framework developed by the New Zealand Qualifications Authority under section 436 of the Education and Training Act 2020; or
the person is engaged in study of a kind approved by MSD, and was so engaged when the person became subject to the work test; or
the person is engaged in full-time study of a kind approved by MSD; or
the person has primary responsibility for the day-to-day care of a child, other than on a temporary basis, and the child—
is aged less than 3 years; and
is not a dependent child of that person.

history.history-note: Regulation 104(2)﻿(d): amended, on 1 August 2022, by section 74 of the Education and Training Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 38).
Regulation 104(2)﻿(d): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

subpart.heading: Exemption from obligations under Act of certain young people

prov.heading: Exemption from obligations under section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of Act: youth support payment recipients and spouses and partners of specified beneficiaries

para.text: Any young person who is subject to obligations under section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of the Act may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of those obligations on any ground specified in subclause (2).
A person referred to in subclause (1) may be granted an exemption from some or all of the person’s obligations under section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of the Act on the ground that—
the person has recently changed the person’s living circumstances because of family violence and needs time to adjust to the change; or
the person would qualify for a supported living payment on the ground of restricted work capacity or total blindness under section 34 of the Act; or
the person would meet the criteria (except for the person’s age) for jobseeker support on the health condition, injury, or disability ground in section 21(1)﻿(b) or 22(c) of the Act; or
the person is caring full-time for the person’s spouse or partner (who has a health condition, an injury, or a disability of a kind that requires that person to be cared for full-time); or
the person is serving a sentence of home detention under the Sentencing Act 2002; or
the person’s capacity to meet the person’s obligations under section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of the Act is limited because the person is subject to—
a sentence of supervision under the Sentencing Act 2002; or
release conditions (as that term is defined in section 4(1) of the Parole Act 2002); or
conditions of release on bail under any enactment; or
any other sentence or order imposed by a court.

prov.heading: Exemption from obligations under section 162 or 164 of Act: youth support payment recipients who have been in relationship

para.text: This regulation applies to any young person who receives—
a young parent payment; or
a youth payment.

Any young person to whom this regulation applies may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of the person’s obligations under section 162 or 164 of the Act on the ground that the person needs time to adjust because—
the person’s spouse or partner has recently died; or
the person has recently separated from or ceased to live together with the person’s spouse or partner; or
the person’s de facto relationship has recently ended.

prov.heading: Exemption from obligations under section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of Act: young parent payment recipients, and young persons with dependent children who are spouses or partners of specified beneficiaries

para.text: This regulation applies to any young person who is subject to obligations under section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of the Act because—
the young person receives a young parent payment; or
section 167(1) of the Act applies.

Any young person to whom this regulation applies may apply under section 158 of the Act to be exempted from some or all of the person’s obligations under section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of the Act on the ground that—
the person is the principal caregiver of a dependent child or foster child with special needs (as defined in regulation 99)—

whose welfare would be unduly harmed if the person were required to comply with an obligation of the kind referred to in section 162, 164, 166, or 167 of the Act that requires full-time attendance; and
for whom satisfactory alternative care arrangements are not available to, or are unable to be made by, the caregiver; or
the person is aged 18 years and has primary responsibility for the day-to-day care of a foster child, other than temporarily, and the foster child is aged less than 12 months.

part.heading: Factors affecting benefits

prov.heading: Guide to Part 4

para.text: Part 4 deals with the following topics:
subpart 1 (regulations 109 to 112) deals with pre-benefit activities:
subpart 2 (regulations 113 to 117) deals with the effect of insurance recovery:
subpart 3 (regulations 118 to 123) deals with the effect of an entitlement to overseas pensions:
subpart 4 (regulations 124 to 136) deals with overseas pension deduction:
subpart 6 (regulations 139 to 153) deals with factors affecting benefits: absence from New Zealand:
subpart 7 (regulations 154 to 162) deals with beneficiaries excluded from provisions about the effect of a warrant for a beneficiary’s arrest.

history.history-note: Regulation 108(e): revoked, on 1 April 2020, by section 13(2)﻿(a) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).

prov.heading: Who can MSD require to undertake activities?

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Applicant for certain financial assistance

para.text: Regulation 110 applies to a person who contacts MSD requesting financial assistance under the Act, if MSD considers that the appropriate financial assistance would be a benefit that is—
jobseeker support; or
sole parent support; or
an emergency benefit.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Applicant’s spouse or partner

para.text: Regulation 110 also applies to the spouse or partner of a person who contacts MSD requesting financial assistance under the Act, if the spouse or partner is not, in the spouse’s or partner’s own right, receiving or applying for a main benefit under the Act (as defined in Schedule 2 of the Act), New Zealand superannuation, or a veteran’s pension, and MSD considers that the appropriate financial assistance would be a benefit that is—
jobseeker support; or
a supported living payment on the ground of restricted work capacity or total blindness; or
an emergency benefit.

prov.heading: Requirements related to activities

para.text: MSD may, for the purpose stated in section 3(a)﻿(ii) of the Act, require a person to whom this section applies (under regulation 109(1) or (2)), unless MSD has determined that the person does not have the capacity to seek, undertake, and be available for part-time work, to do any or all of the following:
undertake 1 or more stated pre-benefit activities:

at a time before the benefit to which regulation 109(1) or (2) applies commences, attend and participate in any interview for an opportunity of suitable employment to which the person is referred by MSD:

at a time before the benefit to which regulation 109(1) or (2) applies commences, accept any offer of suitable employment (whether or not the offer results from an interview of the kind described in paragraph (b)).
A requirement under subclause (1) may be oral or written and, in the case only of a requirement under subclause (1)﻿(a), may be revoked orally or in writing in accordance with subclause (4).
A pre-benefit activity stated under subclause (1)﻿(a) must be available for the person to undertake not later than 10 working days after the date of first contact.
MSD may revoke a requirement that the person undertake a pre-benefit activity under subclause (1)﻿(a) if, and only if, MSD considers the requirement is no longer reasonable.

Pre-benefit activity, in this regulation and regulations 111 and 112 and section 184 of the Act, means an activity that is not an activity in the community (as defined in Schedule 2 of the Act) but is all or any of the following:
reporting on the progress of job-search activities:
completing a curriculum vitae and providing it to MSD:
attending and participating in an employment-related seminar provided by MSD or by any other person approved by MSD for the purpose:
attending and participating in employment-related training approved by MSD for the purpose:
attending and participating in any interview with an MSD employee:
completing any self-assessment or planning required by MSD.
Suitable employment, for the purposes of subclause (1),—
