para.text: If a deduction notice is issued to an employer of a debtor, the employer must not, in making deductions under the deduction notice, reduce the amount paid to the debtor by way of salary or wages in respect of any week to an amount that is less than 60% of the amount calculated as being the debtor’s net ordinary weekly pay for a week.

The debtor’s net ordinary weekly pay for a week, for the purposes of this regulation, is the balance left after deducting from the debtor’s ordinary weekly pay (as defined in section 8 of the Holidays Act 2003) the amount of tax required to be withheld or deducted in accordance with the PAYE rules of the Income Tax Act 2007 if that ordinary weekly pay were the only salary or wages paid to the debtor by the employer in respect of a week.
This regulation overrides anything to the contrary in regulations 216 to 223, 225, and 226.

prov.heading: Variation or discharge of deduction notice

para.text: This regulation applies to a debtor’s payer to whom a deduction notice is issued, or a debtor, who thinks a deduction notice has been issued in error or contains an error.
The debtor’s payer or debtor may bring the matter to the notice of MSD.

If the matter is not resolved to the debtor’s payer’s or debtor’s satisfaction within 5 working days after the day on which the debtor’s payer or debtor brings it to the notice of MSD, the debtor’s payer or debtor may apply (without notice to MSD) to a Registrar of the District Court for the variation or discharge of the notice.
The Registrar may vary or discharge the notice if satisfied that an error has been made, and that the notice ought to be varied or discharged.
The variation or discharge takes effect when notice of it is given under regulation 238 to the debtor’s payer or debtor.
The Registrar must immediately send MSD a copy of the variation or discharge of the notice.

prov.heading: Penalty for late deductions

para.text: This regulation applies to a debtor’s payer to whom a deduction notice has been issued if the debtor’s payer fails wholly or in part to—
make a deduction required to be made by the notice; or
pay an amount deducted under the notice to MSD by the time specified in the notice.
The debtor’s payer is liable to pay to MSD a penalty calculated as follows:
on the amount in default, 10% of that amount or $5, whichever is the greater:

for each additional month or part of a month during which the amount in default or a part of it has not been deducted or, as the case may be, has not been paid to MSD, a further penalty of 2% of the amount or part in default, or $1, whichever is the greater.
MSD may remit all or part of the penalty payable, or may refund all or part of the penalty paid, to MSD under this regulation if satisfied—
that the failure to make the deduction or make the payment was due to circumstances reasonably beyond the debtor’s payer’s control; or
that the imposition of that penalty would in all circumstances be inequitable.

A penalty payable to MSD under this regulation is a debt due to the Crown for the purpose of regulation 206(f), and subject to recovery under section 362 of the Act, from the debtor’s payer as if the penalty were money payable under a benefit to which the debtor’s payer was not entitled.

subpart.heading: Debt recovery suspension

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires,—
benefit debt means a debt due to the Crown (other than a debt that is an emergency housing contribution), and subject to recovery, under section 362(1) (MSD’s duty to recover debts) of the Act
cash assets has the same meaning as in section 68(2) of the Act
deduction notice means a deduction notice issued under regulation 217
full-time course has the same meaning as in section 2(1) of the Student Allowances Regulations 1998
holiday employment means employment undertaken by a full-time student—

after the end of a full-time course in which the student was enrolled in one academic year and before the start of a full-time course in which the student is, or is to be, enrolled in the next academic year; or

after the end of a full-time course in which the student was enrolled in one academic year and before the start of another full-time course in which the student is, or is to be, enrolled in that year; or
after the end of a portion of a full-time course in which the student is enrolled in one academic year and before the resumption in that year of the rest of that course
notice revoking the deduction notice, in relation to a deduction notice, means a written notice—
given by MSD in accordance with regulation 218(3); and
revoking the deduction notice
period of suspension has the meaning given to it by regulation 233
permanent employment position means a position of employment that is—
full employment or full-time employment; but
not temporary employment
person eligible to request suspension has the meaning given to it by regulation 228
request for suspension has the meaning given to it in regulation 231

suspension deadline, in relation to a person eligible to request suspension, means the close of the day that is 3 months after the date on which the person commences employment in a permanent employment position
working-age benefit means a benefit that is—
jobseeker support; or
sole parent support; or
an emergency benefit (other than one payable under section 17(2)﻿(c) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001); or
a youth payment; or
a young parent payment; or
a supported living payment.

history.history-note: Regulation 227 benefit debt: amended, on 30 March 2020, by regulation 8 of the Social Security (Emergency Housing and Transitional Housing) Amendment Regulations 2020 (LI 2020/20).

subpart.crosshead: Eligibility to request debt recovery suspension

prov.heading: Person eligible to request suspension defined

para.text: A person eligible to request suspension means a person—
who is, or is treated as, a person who complies with regulation 229 (which specifies work status and associated requirements); and
who complies with regulation 230 (which specifies cash assets, income, and associated requirements).

prov.heading: Work status and associated requirements

para.text: A person complies with this regulation if—
the person is 18 years old or older; and
MSD is satisfied that the person has entered, or is to enter, into a permanent employment position; and
the person complies with subclause (2).
A person complies with this subclause if—
the person is, or is treated as, a person receiving a working-age benefit; or
the person has been granted a working-age benefit that, under section 313 of the Act, is subject to a stand down and commences on a date calculated in accordance with section 316 of the Act; or
the person is a full-time student who is not undertaking holiday employment; or
the person—
is not, and is not treated as, a person receiving a working-age benefit; and
has moved or is moving from a permanent employment position or position of employment of some other kind (the first position) to a permanent employment position (the second position); and
has commenced or is to commence employment in the second position after (but not more than 4 weeks after) the person’s employment in the first position terminated or is to terminate; or
the person—
is not, and is not treated as, a person receiving a working-age benefit; and
is a person who MSD is satisfied is re-entering or has re-entered the workforce.
MSD may for the purposes of these regulations treat a person younger than 18 years as a person who complies with this regulation if—
the person complies with subclause (1)﻿(b) and (c); and
MSD considers there are special circumstances that warrant that treatment.

prov.heading: Cash assets, income, and associated requirements

para.text: A person complies with this regulation if MSD is satisfied that the person has entered, or is to enter, into a permanent employment position and—
the person has cash assets (including the cash assets of the person’s spouse or partner, if any) of less than,—
for a single person, the amount in clause 1 of Schedule 5 of the Act; or
for a person who is in a relationship (with or without dependent children), or is a sole parent (with 1 or more dependent children), the amount in clause 2 of Schedule 5 of the Act; and
the person has income (including the income of the person’s spouse or partner, if any) of less than the appropriate amount set out in Part 2 of Schedule 5 of the Act; and

if the person has been granted a working-age benefit that, under section 313 of the Act, is subject to a stand down and commences on a date calculated in accordance with section 316 of the Act, or is, or is treated as, a person receiving a working-age benefit, the person has taken all practicable steps to ensure that the grant of the benefit or, as the case may be, the benefit, is cancelled when the person commenced or is to commence employment in the permanent employment position.

prov.heading: Making and withdrawing requests for suspension

para.text: A person eligible to request suspension may, at any time before the suspension deadline,—
make a request that recovery of the person’s benefit debts be suspended (a request for suspension); or
withdraw a request for suspension the person made.
A request for suspension may be made orally or in writing to MSD but, if made orally, must as soon as practicable be confirmed in writing.
A withdrawal of a request for suspension must be made in writing to MSD.
A request for suspension, or withdrawal of one, must be treated as having been made when it is received by MSD.
A person is not prevented from making or withdrawing a request for suspension just because the person has, in accordance with this regulation,—
made 1 or more earlier requests for suspension; and
withdrawn those 1 or more earlier requests.

prov.heading: Suspension of recovery of benefit debts

para.text: This regulation applies to a person who—
is a person eligible to request suspension; and
has, in accordance with regulation 231, made a request for suspension; and
has not, in accordance with regulation 231, withdrawn the request.
Benefit debts of a person to whom this regulation applies must not, during the period of suspension, be recovered in any of the following ways:
by way of proceedings or deductions, and under subpart 10:
by way of the issuing of, or deductions under, a deduction notice.
As soon as practicable after this regulation starts to apply to a person, MSD must give a notice revoking the deduction notice in respect of every deduction notice that—
relates to benefit debts of the person; and
has not already been revoked or otherwise ceased to have effect.
MSD must take all practicable steps to ensure that a notice given in accordance with subclause (3) is received—
as soon as practicable, if the person has commenced employment in the permanent employment position when the request for suspension is made; and
as soon as practicable after the person commences employment in the permanent employment position, if the person has not commenced employment in that position when the request for suspension is made.

prov.heading: Period of suspension defined

para.text: The period of suspension means the period that—
starts (for amounts other than those referred to in paragraph (b)) at the later of the following times:
the time the relevant request to suspend recovery was made; and
the time the person commenced employment in the permanent employment position; and

starts for amounts to be recovered by way of deductions under a deduction notice at the time (determined in accordance with subpart 10) that the person to whom the deduction notice was issued received a notice revoking the deduction notice; and
ends on the suspension deadline.

subpart.crosshead: How receipt of working-age benefit affects debt recovery suspension

prov.heading: Suspension starts only if benefit has been cancelled

para.text: This regulation applies to a person described in regulation 232(1) who is, or is treated as, a person who is receiving, or has been granted, a working-age benefit.
Recovery of the person’s benefit debts must not be suspended in accordance with regulation 232 unless—
the working-age benefit or, as the case may be, the grant of that benefit, has been cancelled; or
the person is treated as a person who was receiving a working-age benefit that has now been cancelled.
This regulation overrides regulation 232.

prov.heading: Suspension ceases if person receives benefit

para.text: Suspension of the recovery of a person’s benefit debts in accordance with regulation 232 ceases if the person—
receives a working-age benefit; or
is treated as a person receiving a working-age benefit.
This regulation overrides regulation 232.

subpart.crosshead: Certain spouses or partners treated as persons who are or were receiving working-age benefits

prov.heading: Non-qualifying spouses or partners of recipients of New Zealand superannuation or veteran’s pension

para.text: This regulation applies to a person (person A) who is the spouse or partner of another person (person B) who is receiving, in person B’s own right, the whole or a portion of New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension paid at a grandparented rate.
Person A must, unless subclause (3) applies, be treated for the purposes of this subpart as a person receiving a working-age benefit.
Person A must instead be treated for the purposes of this subpart as a person who was receiving a working-age benefit that has now been cancelled if—

person B elects to no longer receive New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension paid at a grandparented rate (as referred to in clause 7(2)﻿(b) of Schedule 1AA of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001); and

because of that election, the appropriate rate of benefit payable to person B is the whole or a portion of New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension paid at a couple rate other than a grandparented rate.
In this regulation,—
couple rate means a rate prescribed for a person who is in a relationship
grandparented rate means—
a rate specified in clause 1 of Part 2 of Schedule 1 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001; or
the relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate specified in regulations referred to in section 169 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014.

history.history-note: Regulation 236(1): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 52(1) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Regulation 236(3): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 52(2) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Regulation 236(4): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 52(2) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Spouses or partners of persons granted working-age benefits payable at certain rates

para.text: This regulation applies to a person (person A) who is the spouse or partner of another person (person B) if person B has been granted a working-age benefit payable at the rate for a person whose spouse or partner has not been granted a benefit in the spouse’s or partner’s own right.
Person A must, unless subclause (3) applies, be treated for the purposes of this subpart as a person receiving a working-age benefit.

If person A commences employment in a permanent employment position and the rate of benefit payable is abated, on account of the income from the employment, to half, or less than half, of the rate referred to in subclause (1), person A must instead be treated for the purposes of this subpart as a person who was receiving a working-age benefit that has now been cancelled.

subpart.heading: Requirements to give notice or other document

prov.heading: How MSD or person can meet requirement to give written notice or other document

para.text: This regulation specifies ways in which MSD or a person can meet a requirement in the Act or these regulations to give to another person or MSD a written notice or other document.
The written notice or other document may be given to an individual (other than an officer or employee in the service of the Crown in that person’s official capacity)—
personally in hard copy form; or

by leaving it in hard copy form at that person’s usual or last known place of residence or business or at the address specified by that person in any application or other document received from that person; or
by posting it in hard copy form in a letter addressed to that person at that place of residence or business or at that address; or
by an electronic means (with the individual’s express or inferred consent) in accordance with Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.
The written notice or other document may be given to any other person, including an officer or employee in the service of the Crown in that person’s official capacity,—
if applicable, personally in hard copy form; or
by leaving it in hard copy form at that person’s place of business; or
by posting it in hard copy form in a letter addressed to that person at that place of business; or
by an electronic means (with the person’s express or inferred consent) in accordance with Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.

Written notices or other documents given by post are, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, taken to have been received by the person on the fourth day after the date on which the letter was posted (and, in proving postal delivery, it is enough to prove the letter was properly addressed and posted).
This regulation—
is subject to section 254 of the Act (how notice of sanction may be given); and
operates under sections 392(2), 400(2), and 412(2) of the Act.

prov.heading: Rates and conditions of employment and payment: prescribed health practitioners

para.text: A health practitioner who is any of the following, acting within their scope of practice, is prescribed for the purposes of section 379 of the Act:
a medical practitioner:
a psychologist:
a dentist:
a midwife:
a nurse practitioner.

subpart.heading: Reciprocity agreements: requirements and process

prov.heading: Requirements for agreements with mutual assistance provisions

para.text: A reciprocity agreement may contain and include mutual assistance provisions of 1 or both of the following kinds:
provisions for the Government of New Zealand and the Government of another country to provide each other with assistance in the recovery of social security debts:
provisions for those Governments to supply each other with information for social security purposes.
No mutual assistance provision of the kind referred to in subclause (1)﻿(a) may be contained and included in a reciprocity agreement unless the agreement is subject to—
the terms and conditions set out in regulation 241; or
terms and conditions that are to an equivalent effect.
No mutual assistance provision of the kind referred to in subclause (1)﻿(b) may be contained and included in a reciprocity agreement unless the agreement is subject to—
the terms and conditions set out in regulation 242; or
terms and conditions that are to an equivalent effect.

prov.heading: Agreement terms and conditions for debt recovery

para.text: The terms and conditions referred to in regulation 240(2)﻿(a) are as follows:
assistance to recover any social security debt of a party may be provided by the other party only in respect of a debt—

that has been found or determined to be owing in the country concerned by a court or tribunal having jurisdiction in the matter, or by a person, a body, or an organisation in that country acting administratively within the terms of a lawful authorisation; and

in respect of which any right of review of or appeal against the determination of the debt, under the law under which the debt was determined (other than a right of judicial review or complaint under laws relating to administrative decisions generally, or under human rights laws),—
has been exhausted or has expired; or
if there is no time limit for the exercise of any such right, has not been exercised, or has been exercised and the review or appeal has been finally determined; and
that may be lawfully recovered under the laws of that country; and
that was first found or determined to be owing less than 5 years before the date that the request for assistance is made, except as provided in subclause (3):
when providing assistance to recover any social security debt of a party, the party giving the assistance is not required to—
give priority to the recovery of social security debts of the other party; and
take any measures for recovery not provided for under the laws relating to the recovery of debts of that party:
a party cannot seek to recover a social security debt by imprisonment of the individual by whom it is owed or of any other individual:
any recovery of a social security debt of a deceased individual is limited to the value of that individual’s estate:
any institution, court, or tribunal involved in the recovery of a social security debt may defer recovery of the debt, or may order or arrange for the debt to be paid in instalments, if—
the institution, court, or tribunal has the power to do so; and
it is its normal practice to do so:
a party may give assistance only in respect of a social security debt that the requesting institution has certified is of a kind described in paragraph (a)﻿(i) to (iv):

if a party gives assistance in respect of a social security debt for which there is no time limit for exercising a right of review of or appeal against the determination of the debt under the law under which the debt was determined, or for which there is a time limit but a right of review or appeal may be exercised after its expiry in the discretion of the court or tribunal or body or person concerned, or in the discretion of any other person,—
the requesting institution must immediately advise the requested institution if a right of that kind is exercised; and
the requested institution must defer recovery of the debt until notified by the requesting institution that the review or appeal is finally determined:

the party requesting assistance to recover a social security debt must pay the costs of the other party of recovering the debt, including court costs or other fees payable under the laws of that party.
For the purposes of subclause (1)﻿(a)﻿(ii), a right of review or appeal under the law under which a debt was determined has expired—
if the right has not been exercised within the time limit provided for its exercise; and

irrespective of whether the court or tribunal or body or person that would have jurisdiction in respect of the review or appeal, or any other person, retains a discretion (however expressed in that law) to allow the right of review or appeal to be exercised after that time.

If an institution, a court, or a tribunal defers the recovery of a social security debt, or orders or arranges for a social security debt to be paid by instalments, the 5-year period referred to in subclause (1)﻿(a)﻿(iv) is extended by the period of deferral or, as the case requires, the period over which the debt is to be paid by instalments.

prov.heading: Agreement terms and conditions for information exchange

para.text: The terms and conditions referred to in regulation 240(3)﻿(a) are as follows:
a request for information relating to an individual may be made only for social security purposes:
the requested institution of a party may supply to the requesting institution any information about the person to whom the request relates that it holds or is lawfully able to obtain:

the requesting institution of a party (the requesting party) to whom information is supplied by the requested institution of the other party may supply that information to the taxation authorities of the requesting party for either or both of the following purposes:
making an assessment of the tax due by any person under the laws of the requesting party relating to taxation:
detecting tax fraud or tax evasion under the laws of the requesting party:
every request for and supply of information made by and to the competent institutions of the parties must be made in terms of an agreement between the competent institutions of the parties that—
specifies the types of information that the competent institutions may supply to each other; and
limits the supply of information to the types of information specified; and

in relation to New Zealand, contains, with all necessary modifications, the safeguards (subject to subparagraph (iv)) required to be set out in an information matching agreement within the meaning of section 178 of the Privacy Act 2020; and
in relation to New Zealand, requires the information matching rules set out in clause 3 of Schedule 6 of the Privacy Act 2020, with all necessary modifications, to be applied; and
in relation to New Zealand, has been agreed to by the Privacy Commissioner, the Commissioner having had regard to the matters in section 381(2)﻿(a) of the Act:

any information supplied by a party to the other party must be subject to the same privacy protections (subject to paragraphs (b) and (c)) as any other personal information obtained under the social security laws of the other party:

no party that receives, under the agreement, personal information about any individual from the competent institution of the other party may supply that information to any other country without the prior written consent of that competent institution or the individual concerned:

a party must supply the competent institution of the other party with any information required by that institution to answer any questions or to make any report or return required by a person or body authorised to monitor compliance with that party’s privacy laws.

In relation to New Zealand, section 178(3) of the Privacy Act 2020 applies, with any necessary modifications, to an agreement under subclause (1)﻿(d) between the competent institutions of the parties.

history.history-note: Regulation 242(1)﻿(d)﻿(iii): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Regulation 242(1)﻿(d)﻿(iv): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Regulation 242(1)﻿(d)﻿(v): replaced, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Regulation 242(2): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

subpart.crosshead: Adverse action arising from discrepancy

prov.heading: Adverse action against individual if discrepancy shown by information from other country

para.text: This regulation applies if information received by MSD from the competent institution of another country under a mutual assistance provision of the kind referred to in regulation 240(1)﻿(b) has produced a discrepancy, and as a result MSD proposes to take an adverse action against an individual.
MSD must give that individual written notice—
specifying particulars of the discrepancy and of the adverse action MSD proposes to take; and
stating that the individual has 5 working days from the receipt of the notice in which to show cause why that action should not be taken.
MSD cannot take an adverse action against an individual to whom a notice has been sent until the expiry of the 5 working days referred to in subclause (2)﻿(b).

Subclauses (2) and (3) do not prevent MSD from taking adverse action against an individual if compliance with the requirements of subclause (2) would prejudice an investigation into the commission, or the possible commission, of an offence.
A written notice to be given under subclause (2) may be given as specified in regulations made under the Privacy Act 2020 for the giving of notices under section 181 of that Act.

A failure by MSD to comply, in relation to an individual, with subclause (2) is taken for the purposes of Part 5 of the Privacy Act 2020, to constitute a failure to comply with the provisions of subpart 4 of Part 7 of that Act.
In this regulation and regulation 244, expressions defined in section 177 of the Privacy Act 2020 have the meanings so defined, with any necessary modifications.

history.history-note: Regulation 243(5): replaced, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Regulation 243(6): replaced, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Regulation 243(7): replaced, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Exception if specified information exchange provision produces unique identifier discrepancy

para.text: This regulation applies if information received by MSD from the competent institution of another country under an information exchange provision to which subclause (4) applies—

contains identifying information about an individual (including the unique identifier assigned to that individual by that institution) who has applied for a benefit payable under the agreement in either country; and
produces a discrepancy with information already held by MSD about that person.
MSD must give that individual written notice (unless MSD has previously given notice under this subclause to that individual, whether for the discrepancy or another discrepancy)—
specifying particulars of the discrepancy and of the action MSD proposes to take as a result of that discrepancy; and
specifying the unique identifiers assigned to that individual by the competent institutions of both countries; and
informing that individual that information received from the other country about that individual will be matched with information in MSD’s possession using those unique identifiers; and
specifying the kinds of information about the individual that MSD is likely to receive from the other country under the agreement; and
specifying the kinds of actions that MSD may take as a result of information about that individual received at any later time from the other country under the agreement; and
specifying the likely consequences of those actions for any benefit payable to that individual under the agreement or otherwise; and

stating that the individual has 5 working days from the receipt of the notice to show cause why MSD should not take the action referred to in paragraph (a) or actions of the kinds referred to in paragraph (e).
After the 5 working days referred to in subclause (2)﻿(g), MSD need not comply with regulation 243(2) and (3) in respect of any information that is—
later received from the other country under the provision; and
received for a purpose set out in subclause (4); and
matched against information held by MSD using the unique identifiers assigned to the individual concerned by the competent institutions of both countries.

This subclause applies to a provision that is contained in a reciprocity agreement, and that is a provision of the kind referred to in regulation 240(1)﻿(b), but only if the social security purposes concerned are wholly or principally the administration of the agreement itself, and the parties to the agreement are likely often to exchange information about individuals for all or any of the following purposes:
granting or paying benefits under the agreement:
calculating the rates of benefits payable under the agreement:
calculating the rates of other benefits that are affected by the rates of benefits payable under the agreement.

part.heading: Reviews and appeals

prov.heading: Guide to Part 7

para.text: Part 7 deals with the following topics:
reviews by a benefits review committee:
appeals to an appeal authority:
appeals to the High Court by way of case stated:
appeals to the medical board.

prov.heading: Application under section 391 of Act to be made in writing

para.text: An application for review under section 391 of the Act must be made in writing.

prov.heading: Procedure on review

para.text: The committee must, as soon as practicable after it receives an application for review, review the decision.
In reviewing the decision, the committee may, in accordance with the Act, decide to confirm, vary, or revoke the decision.
The committee must give written notice of its decision on the review to the applicant for review.

The written notice must include the reasons for the decision, and advice that the applicant has, under sections 395 to 399 of the Act, a right to appeal to the appeal authority against the decision if the committee has, under this regulation, confirmed or varied MSD’s decision.

prov.heading: Appeal is begun by written notice of appeal lodged within 3 months of notification or further allowed period

para.text: An appeal under section 397, 398, or 399 of the Act is begun by a written notice of appeal.
The notice of appeal must state with particularity the grounds of appeal and the relief sought.
The notice of appeal must be lodged with the Secretary of the appeal authority within the period allowed by section 400 of the Act.
The parties to an appeal to the appeal authority are—
the person to whom section 397, 398, or 399 of the Act gives the right to appeal against the decision (the appellant); and
MSD.
Before or immediately after the notice of appeal is lodged, the appellant must provide a copy of it to MSD.

prov.heading: Material that MSD must send to authority

para.text: MSD must, as soon as possible after receiving the copy of the notice of appeal, send to the Secretary of the appeal authority copies of the following:
any application, documents, written submissions, statements, reports, and other papers lodged with, received by, or prepared for, MSD and relating to the decision appealed against:
a copy of any notes made by or by direction of MSD of the evidence given at the hearing (if any) before MSD:
any exhibits in the custody of MSD:
a copy of the decision appealed against:
a report setting out the matters considered in making the decision.
The authority may direct that MSD send a further report to the Secretary of the appeal authority (as well as the report sent under subclause (1)﻿(e)).
MSD must give or send promptly to each other party to the appeal a copy of every report or further report sent under subclause (1)﻿(e) or (2).
Each other party to the appeal is entitled to be heard and to offer evidence on a matter referred to in a report or further report sent under subclause (1)﻿(e) or (2).
Subclause (4) is subject to regulation 250(3).

prov.heading: Fixing time and place for hearing of appeal

para.text: The appeal authority must, as soon as convenient after receiving a notice of appeal,—
fix a time and place for the hearing of the appeal; and
give the appellant and MSD a reasonable period of notice of the hearing.
The reasonable period of notice must be at least 10 days (excluding both the day of giving the notice and the day of the hearing).
Subclause (1) does not apply if the authority considers that the appeal can be properly determined without a hearing.

prov.heading: Expenses payable to appellants

para.text: This regulation applies to an appellant whom the appeal authority requests to appear before it if the appellant does so.
MSD must, out of money appropriated by Parliament for the purpose, pay the actual and reasonable travelling and accommodation expenses (if any) incurred by the appellant.

prov.heading: Hearing of appeal: representation

para.text: At the hearing of any appeal,—
MSD may be represented by counsel or by an MSD employee; and
any other party may appear and act personally or by counsel or any duly authorised representative.

prov.heading: Hearing of appeal: evidence

para.text: Every appeal to the appeal authority against a decision of MSD must be by way of rehearing; but if any question of fact is involved in any appeal, the relevant evidence taken before or received by MSD must, subject to any special order, be brought before the authority as follows:
in respect of any evidence given orally, by the production of a copy of the notes of MSD or of any other material the authority thinks expedient:

in respect of any evidence taken by affidavit and any exhibits, by the production of the affidavits and any exhibits that may have been sent to the authority by MSD, and by the production by the parties to the appeal of any exhibits in their custody.
Subclause (1) is subject to regulation 250(3).
On an appeal against a decision of MSD, despite subclause (1), the authority—
may rehear all or any part of the evidence; and
must rehear the evidence of a witness if the authority has reason to believe that any note made by MSD of the evidence of that witness is or may be incomplete in a material particular.

The authority has full discretionary power to hear and receive evidence or further evidence on questions of fact, whether by oral evidence, by affidavit, or by evidence given by other written means or by electronic means.

The authority must also have regard to any report lodged by MSD under regulation 249 and to any matters referred to in it and to any evidence offered on it, whether or not those matters would be otherwise admissible in evidence.

In the exercise of its powers under this regulation, the authority may receive as evidence any statement, document, information, or matter that, in the authority’s opinion, may assist it to deal with the matters before it, whether or not the same would be admissible in a court of law.

prov.heading: Determination of appeal

para.text: The authority may confirm, modify, or reverse the decision appealed against.
Subclause (1) enables the authority to exercise or perform only powers, duties, functions, and discretions that MSD had in respect of the same matter.
Despite subclause (1), the authority may refer to MSD for further consideration all or a part of the matter to which an appeal relates.

The authority must advise MSD of its reasons for the referral and give any directions it thinks just in respect of the rehearing or reconsideration or otherwise of all or a part of the matter referred.

prov.heading: Costs award or order in favour of, or against, appellant

para.text: If an appeal is allowed in whole or in part, or all or a part of the matter is referred back to MSD, the authority may allow the appellant the costs of bringing all or a part of the appeal.
If an appeal is not allowed, no award of costs can be made against the appellant unless, in the authority’s opinion, the appeal was frivolous or vexatious or ought not to have been brought.
The amount of costs awarded must be stated in the order.

If an award of costs is made under this regulation and any sum remains unpaid, the person in whose favour the award or order was made may obtain from the Secretary of the authority a certificate under seal of the sum awarded. If the certificate is filed in the District Court, it may be enforced in respect of the amount specified in it (if any) that is still owing as if it were a judgment of that court.

prov.heading: MSD may be required to pay authority’s costs

para.text: This regulation applies if an appeal is allowed in whole or in part and the authority has incurred costs in hearing and determining the appeal.
The authority may, if it considers it appropriate, require MSD to pay a sum fixed by the authority to cover all or part of those costs.

part.crosshead: Appeals to High Court by way of case stated

prov.heading: Appeal is begun by notice of appeal lodged within period required by section 406 of Act

para.text: An appellant begins an appeal under section 405 of the Act by lodging a written notice of appeal with the Secretary of the authority within the period allowed by section 406 of the Act.
The appellant must promptly deliver or post a copy of the lodged notice of appeal to every other party to the proceedings.

prov.heading: Appellant must state, and lodge with authority, case stated

para.text: The appellant must state in writing, and lodge with the Secretary of the authority, a case stated for the court’s opinion on a question of law only.
The appellant’s written case stated must be lodged within the period allowed by section 406(2) or (3) of the Act.
The appellant’s written case stated must set out the facts and the grounds of the determination, and must specify the question of law on which the appeal is made.
The appellant must promptly deliver or post a copy of the lodged written case stated to every other party to the proceedings.
If the appellant does not lodge a case stated under section 406(2), (3), or (4) of the Act, the authority’s chairperson may certify that the appeal has not been prosecuted.

prov.heading: Statement by authority of case stated lodged by appellant

para.text: The Secretary of the authority must, as soon as practicable after the lodging of the appellant’s written case stated, submit it to the authority’s chairperson.
The chairperson must, as soon as practicable, settle the case stated, sign it, send it to the Registrar of the High Court at Wellington, and make a copy available to each party.
The chairperson may, if the chairperson considers it necessary to do so, hear the parties on how the written case stated should be settled by the chairperson.
The authority states the case stated for the court’s opinion when the authority’s chairperson settles and signs the written case stated lodged by the appellant.

prov.heading: Appeal must be dealt with and determined as provided in rules of court

para.text: The appeal must be dealt with and determined (in particular, the High Court must hear and determine the question of law in the case stated) in accordance with the rules of court that are applicable and not inconsistent with Schedule 8 of, or with any other provision of, the Act.

prov.heading: Appeal is begun by notice of appeal lodged within 3 months of notification or further allowed period

para.text: An applicant or a beneficiary begins an appeal under section 411 of the Act to the medical board by lodging a written notice of appeal with the board within the period allowed by section 412 of the Act.
The notice of appeal must state with particularity the grounds of appeal and the relief sought.
Before or immediately after the lodging of the notice of appeal, a copy of it must be provided to MSD.

prov.heading: Travelling expenses and allowances related to medical board

para.text: If any applicant or beneficiary has to be absent from that person’s regular place of residence to appear before a medical board, travelling expenses and allowances may be available to that person under regulation 175.

part.heading: Other provisions

prov.heading: Guide to Part 8

para.text: Part 8 of these regulations deals with various topics as follows:
subpart 1 (regulations 264 to 272) deals with contracts for services:
subpart 2 (regulation 273) deals with authorised agencies:
subpart 3 (regulations 274 to 281) deals with information sharing:
subpart 4 (regulations 282 to 293) deals with the SuperGold Card:
subpart 5 (regulation 294) deals with income exemptions:
subpart 5A (regulations 294A and 294B) specifies additional general provisions child support payments:
subpart 6 (regulation 295) deals with revocations.

history.history-note: Regulation 263(ea): inserted, on 22 August 2023, by regulation 4 of the Social Security (Additional General Provisions Child Support Payments) Amendment Regulations 2023 (SL 2023/188).

subpart.heading: Contracts for services
subpart.crosshead: Interpretation

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires,—
personal information means personal information about a person or persons to whom a section 373 services contract applies and that is held by the contracted service provider concerned or MSD.

section 373 services contract means a contract entered into under section 373 of the Act for the provision of services of the kind referred to in all or any of section 373(1)﻿(a)﻿(i)﻿(A), (a)﻿(i)﻿(B), and (b) of the Act.

subpart.crosshead: Contracts for services for young people

prov.heading: Services to encourage and help young people to move into or remain in education, training, and employment

para.text: The following services, which are services of the kind referred to in sections 365(1)﻿(a) and 373(1)﻿(a)﻿(i)﻿(A) of the Act, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(a)﻿(ii) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract):
services relating to the enrolment of young people in the contracted youth service, including obtaining their agreement to participate in the service and their consent to disclosure of information:
services relating to assessing the needs of young people participating in the contracted youth service:
services relating to producing, maintaining, and monitoring plans about the educational, training, and employment goals for young people participating in the contracted youth service:
services relating to providing ongoing support, guidance, and mentoring to young people participating in the contracted youth service:
services relating to referring young people participating in the contracted youth service to education, training, work placement, and other developmental opportunities:
services relating to facilitating access to other services to address barriers that prevent young people from successfully moving back into education, training, or employment:

services relating to young people who are parents to help them access approved early childhood education (ECE) and make them aware of the benefits of ECE or other suitable childcare while participating in education, training, or employment.

prov.heading: Services in relation to financial support for young people

para.text: The following services, which are services of the kind referred to in section 373(1)﻿(a)﻿(i)﻿(B) of the Act, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(a)﻿(ii) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract):

services relating to assisting young people to apply for any benefit or financial assistance under the Act and, as the case requires, providing recommendations to MSD in respect of any application for any benefit or financial assistance under the Act:
services relating to conducting assessments to inform decisions as to a young person’s eligibility for youth support payments:

services relating to identifying and quantifying a young person’s accommodation costs, service costs, and lawful debts and liabilities, and recommending that the young person’s payments be paid in a youth money management manner of payment under section 341(3)﻿(d) of the Act and regulation 194(1)﻿(a) and (b):
services relating to recommending to MSD amounts for the purposes of regulation 194(1)﻿(c) and (d):
services relating to assisting young people to develop money management skills and the monitoring of a young person’s expenditure and reporting to MSD:
services relating to assessing the needs of young people and, where applicable, the needs of their dependent children for services or interventions:

services relating to assisting, monitoring, and reporting to MSD on compliance by young people with their obligations under Part 3 of the Act (including obligations under section 164(1) of the Act relating to dependent children), and recommending to MSD whether they have failed to comply with any obligation without good and sufficient reason or are eligible to receive an incentive payment:
services relating to producing, maintaining, and monitoring plans about the educational, training, and employment goals for young people:

services relating to delivering or purchasing budgeting programmes or parenting education programmes for young people to undertake in compliance with their obligations under section 162(1)﻿(b) or 164(1)﻿(a) of the Act:

services relating to providing young people with, or facilitating their access to, appropriate services and other assistance in connection with their circumstances (for example, drug or alcohol addiction, health issues (including mental health issues)):
services relating to assessing whether young people are paying reasonable accommodation costs, and providing advice about, and assistance in, reducing those costs where possible:

services relating to young people who are parents to help them access approved early childhood education (ECE) and make them aware of the benefits of ECE or other suitable childcare while they are in education, training, work-based learning, or part-time employment:
services relating to monitoring a young person’s participation in part-time work:
services relating to acting as MSD’s agent to receive notifications of a young person’s change of circumstances affecting eligibility for, or the rate of, any benefit.

history.history-note: Regulation 266(c): amended, on 26 May 2025, by section 75 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

subpart.crosshead: Contracts for services for people other than young people

prov.heading: Services in relation to work-preparation obligations

para.text: The services set out in subclause (2), which are services in relation to all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(b) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract) if—
the contract is for the provision of services to beneficiaries for the purposes of subpart 2 of Part 2, sections 120 to 126, 138 to 161, and 170(2)﻿(c)﻿(ii), and Part 5 of the Act; and
those beneficiaries are in receipt of a main benefit or are the spouse or partner of a person granted a main benefit; and
those beneficiaries are required to comply with sections 124 and 125 of the Act or section 125 only.
The services are—
services for the active case management of beneficiaries who have work-preparation obligations under section 125 of the Act:
services for carrying out an individualised needs assessment for each beneficiary:
services for planning for the preparation of a beneficiary for employment:
services for assisting a beneficiary to arrange and attend an interview for employment:
services for maintaining relationships between a beneficiary and the beneficiary’s health and social service providers, prospective employers, and training or education providers:
services for assisting, monitoring, or reporting on a beneficiary’s compliance with the beneficiary’s work-preparation obligations under section 125 of the Act:
services that facilitate access to other services that will assist the beneficiary to prepare for employment (such as transport and childcare services):

services for identifying, and helping with, arrangements for overcoming factors that impede a beneficiary’s entering or continuing in employment, or both, including (but not limited to) domestic and social circumstances, health or disability conditions, drug or alcohol use, transport, childcare, and workplace facilities.

prov.heading: Services in relation to social obligations

para.text: The services set out in subclause (2), which are services to beneficiaries in relation to sections 131 to 135 and 170(2)﻿(c)﻿(iii) of the Act, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(b) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract).
The services are—
services for assisting a beneficiary to understand, or to take all reasonable steps to comply with, the following obligations:
enrolling a dependent child in an approved early childhood education programme and ensuring that the child attends that programme:
enrolling a dependent child at a registered school and ensuring that the child attends that school:
enrolling a dependent child with a primary health care provider:
ensuring that a dependent child completes a Well Child check or similar programme:
services for assisting a beneficiary to prepare for and to attend an interview with MSD under section 135(a) of the Act:
services for educating a beneficiary on how to report on the beneficiary’s compliance with the beneficiary’s social obligations under section 135(b) of the Act:
services for assisting, monitoring, or reporting on a beneficiary’s compliance with the beneficiary’s social obligations under sections 131 to 135 of the Act.

prov.heading: Services in relation to work-test obligations

para.text: The services set out in subclause (2), which are services in relation to all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(b) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract) if—
the contract is for the provision of services to beneficiaries for the purposes of all or any of Parts 1 to 6 and section 170(2)﻿(c)﻿(iv) of the Act; and
those beneficiaries are work-tested beneficiaries in receipt of a work-tested benefit.
The services are—
services for the active case management of beneficiaries who have work-test-obligations under section 146 of the Act:
services for carrying out an individualised needs assessment for each beneficiary:
services for developing a plan for a beneficiary to achieve an employment outcome:
services for assisting a beneficiary to arrange and attend an interview for employment:
services for assisting a beneficiary to access activities or services that contribute to the beneficiary’s plan to achieve an employment outcome:
services for maintaining relationships between a beneficiary and the beneficiary’s health and social service providers, prospective employers, and training or education providers:
services that facilitate access to other services that will assist a beneficiary to obtain employment (such as transport and childcare services):

services for identifying, and helping with, arrangements for overcoming factors that impede a beneficiary’s entering or continuing in employment, or both, including (but not limited to) domestic and social circumstances, health or disability conditions, drug or alcohol use, transport, childcare, and workplace facilities.

prov.heading: Non-compulsory services accepted on voluntary basis

para.text: The services set out in subclause (2), which are services in relation to all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act and which are provided to the following people who are not subject to the obligations set out in section 170(2) of the Act but who voluntarily accept the provision of services relating to those obligations, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(b) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract):
beneficiaries in receipt of a main benefit:
the spouse or partner of a beneficiary in receipt of a main benefit:
other persons who are likely to apply for a main benefit if they cannot financially support themselves.
The services are—

services for identifying, and helping with, arrangements for overcoming factors that impede a beneficiary’s entering or continuing in employment, or both, including (but not limited to) domestic and social circumstances, health or disability conditions, drug or alcohol use, transport, childcare, and workplace facilities:
services for assessing whether a person is competent in money management or should be referred to budget services.

prov.heading: Services relating to assessment

para.text: The services set out in subclause (2), which are services in relation to all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(b) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract) if—
the contract is for the provision of services for the purposes of all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act; and
the services are provided in relation to applicants for benefits or persons receiving a benefit described in any of those Parts of the Act.
The services are—
services for assessing a person’s eligibility for a benefit or other allowances or subsidies under the Act:
services for assessing what services, activities, or employment are suitable for a beneficiary:
services for making a work ability assessment or reassessment for the purposes of sections 116 to 118 of the Act.

prov.heading: Services for provision of activities, pre-benefit activities, and budgeting advice

para.text: The services set out in subclause (2), which are services in relation to all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act, are stated for the purposes of section 373(1)﻿(b) of the Act (and so are services for a section 373 services contract) if——
the contract is for the provision of services for the purposes of all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act; and
the services are provided in relation to applicants for benefits or persons receiving a benefit described in all or any of Parts 1 to 6 of the Act.
The services are—
services for the provision of pre-benefit activities for the purpose of section 184 of the Act and of subpart 1 of Part 4 of these regulations:
services for the provision of activities for the purpose of section 125, 146(1)﻿(d), or 324 of the Act:
services for the provision of budgeting advice.

prov.heading: Authorised agencies

para.text: The agencies listed in Schedule 7 of these regulations are authorised for the purposes of clause 18 of Schedule 6 of the Act.
A reference in Schedule 7 to a programme or framework is a reference to the programme or framework with that name as it was immediately before 26 November 2018.
In this regulation and Schedule 7,—
agency has the meaning given by section 7(1) of the Privacy Act 2020

health practitioner means a person who is, or is deemed to be, registered with an authority established or continued by section 114 of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 as a practitioner of a particular health profession
health service has the meaning given by section 5(1) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003
primary health organisation means an organisation funded under the National Primary Health Organisation Service Agreement to provide primary health services to people enrolled with that organisation
registered school has the meaning given by section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020
social worker means a person employed in the speciality of social work by an agency other than the department for the time being responsible for administering the Oranga Tamariki Act 1989
youth worker means a person with a qualification or training in youth work who is employed in that speciality.

history.history-note: Regulation 273(3) agency: amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Regulation 273(3) registered school: amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

subpart.heading: Information sharing for purposes of encouraging young people to move into education, employment, and training

prov.heading: Purpose of information sharing under this subpart

para.text: The information sharing permitted by regulations 276 and 277—
is for the purpose set out in clause 19 of Schedule 6 of the Act in relation to a section 373 services contract (as defined in regulation 264); and
is subject to regulation 281.

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires, personal information means personal information to which clause 20 of Schedule 6 of the Act applies.

prov.heading: Education, employment, and training: circumstances in which contracted service provider may release or disclose to MSD personal information

para.text: A contracted service provider and its employees may release or disclose to MSD and MSD employees personal information in the following circumstances:
when any of the following occurs, for which a report by the contracted service provider to MSD is required in a section 373 services contract (as defined in regulation 264):
the enrolment of a young person in the contracted youth service:
the progress of a young person against the young person’s milestones:
the payment of a success fee under a section 373 services contract (as so defined):
when a young person transfers from the contracted service provider in question to another, so that MSD may disclose relevant information to the new provider:

when a section 373 services contract (as so defined) terminates, so that MSD may disclose relevant information to a new contracted service provider or use the information in the provision of services of the kind referred to in section 365(1)﻿(a) of the Act.

prov.heading: Education, employment, and training: circumstances in which MSD may release or disclose to contracted service provider personal information

para.text: MSD and MSD employees may release or disclose to a contracted service provider personal information in the following circumstances:

when a young person is identified as not being in education, employment, or training, or as at risk of not being in education, employment, or training, so that the contracted service provider may contact the young person to offer services of the kind referred to in section 365(1)﻿(a) of the Act:
when a young person consents to participate in the contracted youth service, provided that—
the information is requested by the contracted service provider; and

the information is relevant to the young person’s involvement in the contracted youth service; for example, information about the young person’s education, involvement with the department responsible for administering the Oranga Tamariki Act 1989, and involvement with Work and Income (which means that part of MSD responsible for administering the Act).

subpart.crosshead: Information sharing for purposes of section 373 services contract

prov.heading: Purpose of information sharing under this subpart

para.text: The information sharing permitted by regulations 279 and 280 is—
for the purpose set out in clause 19 of Schedule 6 of the Act in relation to a section 373 services contract (as defined in regulation 264); and
is subject to regulation 281.

prov.heading: Functions and powers under section 365 of Act: circumstances in which contracted service provider may release or disclose to MSD personal information

para.text: A contracted service provider and its employees may release or disclose to MSD and MSD employees personal information necessary for MSD to perform any function or exercise any power under section 365 of the Act.
The personal information includes (but is not limited to) the following:
information about a young person that is required when applying for any benefit or financial assistance under the Act:
information about a young person’s accommodation costs, service costs, and lawful debts and liabilities:
information about a young person’s compliance with the Act or these regulations (including information relating to reasons for non-compliance):
information about a young person’s ability to manage the young person’s money:
information about changes in the circumstances of a young person that affect the young person’s eligibility for, or the rate of, any benefit.
The information includes information contained in a report by the contracted service provider to MSD, as specified in a section 373 services contract (as defined in regulation 264), relating to—
the progress of a young person against the young person’s milestones:
the progress of a young person entitling the contracted service provider to a success fee:
other services to which a young person has been referred:
the relevant circumstances of a young person on leaving the contracted youth service (including whether the young person or the young person’s partner has given birth to a child).

prov.heading: Section 373 services contract: circumstances in which MSD may release or disclose personal information to contracted service provider

para.text: MSD and MSD employees may release or disclose to a contracted service provider and its employees personal information necessary for the contracted service provider to provide a young person assigned to it with a contracted youth service.
The personal information may include (but is not limited to) the following:
contact details:
relevant information about the young person’s involvement with the department responsible for administering the Oranga Tamariki Act 1989:
relevant information about the young person’s involvement with Work and Income (which means that part of MSD responsible for administering the Act).

subpart.crosshead: Conditions of information sharing

prov.heading: Conditions of information sharing

para.text: Personal information may be released or disclosed under clause 20 of Schedule 6 of the Act if the following conditions are met:
the party releasing or disclosing the information and the party receiving it comply with their obligations under the Privacy Act 2020 and any other applicable legislation; and
the contract—
describes the information or type of information that one party may disclose to the other party; and
states the purposes for which the information may be disclosed; and
states the use that the contracted service provider may make of the information disclosed to it; and
states the use that MSD may make of the information disclosed to it; and
states that the contracted service provider must not disclose the information disclosed to it to any other agency, body, or person unless first authorised by MSD; and

in the case of a section 373 services contract (as defined in regulation 264), states that the contracted service provider must not disclose the information disclosed to it to any other agency, body, or person unless first authorised by the young person to whom the information relates; and
states the form in which the information may be disclosed; and
states the method by which the information may be disclosed; and
states when the contracted service provider must destroy the information; and
includes safeguards regarding the security of the information; and

includes a process to facilitate the fair, simple, speedy, and efficient resolution of a complaint that a young person’s personal information has been released or disclosed in circumstances not authorised by regulation 277 or 279 or has been dealt with in a way not authorised by this regulation; and
includes a process for dealing with any breach of the contract.

history.history-note: Regulation 281(a): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires,—
cardholder means a person who holds a valid SuperGold Card issued to that person
community services card means—
a card issued under regulation 4 of the Health Entitlement Cards Regulations 1993; or

a corresponding card issued under corresponding regulations made or deemed to have been made under either or both of section 92(3) of the New Zealand Public Health and Disability Act 2000 and section 437 of the Social Security Act 2018
dependent child has the meaning given to it by Schedule 2 of the Act, but does not include a child for whom an orphan’s benefit or an unsupported child’s benefit is paid under the Act

pharmacist means a health practitioner who is, or is deemed to be, registered with the Pharmacy Council established by section 114(5) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 as a practitioner of the profession of pharmacy
provider means a provider of services funded under the New Zealand Public Health and Disability Act 2000
SuperGold Card means an entitlement card issued under regulation 283(1)
territorial authority has the same meaning as in section 5(1) of the Local Government Act 2002
valid, in relation to a SuperGold Card, means signed by the person to whom it was issued.

history.history-note: Regulation 282 territorial authority: inserted, on 1 July 2025, by regulation 4 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2025 (SL 2025/121).

prov.heading: Issue and recall of cards

para.text: MSD—
may issue an entitlement card to any eligible person; and
must issue an entitlement card to an eligible person who applies for one.
MSD may—
recall any SuperGold Card:
reissue a recalled SuperGold Card to the person to whom it was issued:
issue a replacement SuperGold Card to a person whose SuperGold Card has been recalled:
issue a new SuperGold Card to any eligible person whose SuperGold Card has expired or is about to expire:
issue a new SuperGold Card bearing a photograph of the cardholder in place of a SuperGold Card not bearing a photograph.
If satisfied that a person’s SuperGold Card has been stolen, damaged, destroyed, or lost, MSD may issue a replacement SuperGold Card to that person.

prov.heading: Commencement and expiry of cards

para.text: A SuperGold Card may state a date on which it expires, and if it does so, it is not valid after that date.
A SuperGold Card may state the date on which it commences, and if it does so, it is not valid before that date.

prov.heading: Form of SuperGold Cards

para.text: A SuperGold Card—
must bear the word SuperGold; and
must have at least 1 side whose background colour is gold or a dull yellow; and
must bear the name of the cardholder; and
must have imprinted or contained on it a signature panel indicating that the signature written or endorsed on it is the signature of the cardholder; and
if issued to a person who is known to meet the service requirement stated in section 161(1)﻿(a) of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014, may bear distinctive marks indicating that fact.
So long as it complies with subclause (1), a SuperGold Card—
may be issued in any form that MSD decides; and
may have imprinted or contained on it any information or wording that MSD decides.
A SuperGold Card—
may bear a number or code indicating the cardholder’s class of eligibility for the card:
may bear a code indicating the cardholder’s entitlement—
to New Zealand superannuation, a veteran’s pension, a community services card, or any other entitlement; or
to 2 or more of those things:
may have on it a magnetic stripe containing any or all of the following information:
the cardholder’s name:
an identifying number assigned by MSD to the cardholder:
a number or code indicating the cardholder’s class of eligibility for the card:
an identifying number assigned to the card:
the commencement and expiry dates of the card:
a code number for mailing purposes:
may have imprinted on it a barcode containing any or all of the information referred to in paragraph (c):
at the election (in accordance with regulation 286) of the cardholder, may have a photograph of the cardholder affixed to or imaged onto it.
Subclause (3) does not limit the generality of subclause (2).

prov.heading: Cardholders may elect to have photograph on card

para.text: A person may elect to have a photograph of that person affixed to or imaged onto a SuperGold Card to be issued to that person.
A person makes an election by completing and signing a written application form provided by MSD for the purpose, and—

having an authorised agent of MSD take a photograph or photographic image of the person, verifying the person’s identity to the satisfaction of the agent, and giving the completed and signed form to the agent; or
if MSD allows that person to do so, either—
giving to MSD a photograph of that person (the authenticity of which must be verified to MSD’s satisfaction) and the completed and signed form; or
having a photograph of that person (the authenticity of which must be verified to MSD’s satisfaction) and the completed and signed form given to MSD by some other person.

prov.heading: Eligibility for SuperGold Card

para.text: A person is eligible for a SuperGold Card if (and only if) the person—
is of or over the age of 65 years, and is residentially qualified for it; or
is under the age of 65 years and, in accordance with regulation 191, is receiving the whole or a portion of one of the following benefits as the spouse or partner of a beneficiary of the benefit:
New Zealand superannuation paid at a rate specified in clause 1 of Part 2 of Schedule 1 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001:
a veteran’s pension paid at the relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate specified in regulations referred to in section 169 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014; or
is under the age of 65 years, and is receiving a veteran’s pension under section 164 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014.

history.history-note: Regulation 287(b): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 53 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Regulation 287(c): revoked, on 9 November 2020, by section 53 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Residential qualification for SuperGold Card

para.text: For the purposes of regulation 287(a), a person is residentially qualified for a SuperGold Card if (and only if) that person is ordinarily resident in New Zealand, and—
is a New Zealand citizen; or
is the holder of a residence class visa granted under the Immigration Act 2009.

prov.heading: Obligations of cardholders

para.text: As soon as a cardholder receives the cardholder’s SuperGold Card, the cardholder must sign it on the panel provided.
As soon as is reasonably practicable after receiving a written demand for its return signed by or on behalf of MSD, a cardholder must return the cardholder’s SuperGold Card to MSD.

A cardholder must immediately destroy a SuperGold Card on written demand for its destruction made by notice signed by or on behalf of MSD and provided to the cardholder in accordance with regulation 292.
A cardholder must make all reasonable efforts to keep the cardholder’s SuperGold Card safe and secure.
Nothing in this regulation prevents a cardholder from returning the cardholder’s SuperGold Card to MSD and asking for it to be cancelled.

prov.heading: Authorised uses of SuperGold Cards

para.text: A cardholder may use the cardholder’s SuperGold Card for any or all of the following purposes:
to provide evidence that the cardholder is eligible for a SuperGold Card:
to provide evidence to MSD that the cardholder has been issued with it and has been assigned the identifying number endorsed on it:
to provide evidence to a territorial authority that the cardholder is eligible for the increased income abatement threshold for rates rebates that is available to cardholders:
to provide evidence of the cardholder’s class of eligibility for it:

if it bears a code indicating the cardholder is eligible for a community services card, to provide evidence to a pharmacist that the cardholder is entitled to a community services card and has been assigned the identifying number the SuperGold Card bears:

if the card bears a code indicating the cardholder is eligible for a community services card, to provide evidence to any of the persons referred to in subclause (2) that the cardholder is eligible for a community services card, and that the cardholder and the cardholder’s dependent children (if any) are eligible for—

any services provided under a service agreement funded under the New Zealand Public Health and Disability Act 2000, other than treatment paid for (in whole or in part) by the Accident Compensation Corporation or an insurer under the Accident Insurance Act 1998; or
any exemption from a charge or part of a charge for any such service; or
any prescribed maximum amount of charge applicable to people of the class or classes of which the cardholder, or the cardholder’s dependent children, form part, in relation to any such service:

if the card bears a code indicating the cardholder is eligible for a community services card, to provide evidence to any of the following persons, for the purposes of receiving a discounted public transport fare (including validation of the fare and of the cardholder’s entitlement to the discount), that the cardholder is eligible for a community services card:
a person collecting fares for, or issuing tickets to, travel on a public transport service, including any ticketing agent; or
a person operating a public transport service; or
any person carrying out the work specified, and for a person specified, in subparagraph (i) or (ii):
to obtain a service, discount, or concession available to cardholders (or to cardholders of a particular kind or description).
The persons referred to in subclause (1)﻿(e) are—
the chief executive of the Ministry of Health:
Health Benefits Limited:
a medical practitioner, specialist, or pharmacist:
Health New Zealand or provider:
a hospital care institution within the meaning of section 58(4) of the Health and Disability Services (Safety) Act 2001:
an employee of a person described in any of paragraphs (a) to (e).

history.history-note: Regulation 290(1)﻿(ba): inserted, on 1 July 2025, by regulation 5 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2025 (SL 2025/121).
Regulation 290(1)﻿(ea): inserted, on 10 October 2022, by regulation 5 of the Social Security Amendment Regulations (No 2) 2022 (SL 2022/251).
Regulation 290(2)﻿(d): amended, on 1 July 2022, by section 104 of the Pae Ora (Healthy Futures) Act 2022 (2022 No 30).

prov.heading: Unauthorised use of SuperGold Cards

para.text: A cardholder must not—
assign, bail, give, lend, mortgage, pledge, rent, transfer, or sell the cardholder’s SuperGold Card to any other person; or
deal with the cardholder’s SuperGold Card in a way that allows any other person to use it without authority; or
use the cardholder’s SuperGold Card after having been served with a notice under regulation 292 demanding its return.
A cardholder must not—
accept any SuperGold Card by way of assignment, bailment, gift, loan, mortgage, pledge, rental, transfer, or sale; or
make use of another person’s SuperGold Card except as provided for in subclause (7) or (8).
Only an employee of MSD or a person described in regulation 290(2) is entitled to demand or request a SuperGold Card as a form of identification of the cardholder.
Only an employee of MSD is entitled to demand or request a SuperGold Card as evidence that the cardholder is eligible for it.

Only the chief executive of a territorial authority or any other officer of the territorial authority that the chief executive has authorised for the purpose is entitled to demand or request a SuperGold Card as evidence that the cardholder is eligible for the increased income abatement threshold for rates rebates that is available to cardholders.
Only the following persons are entitled to demand or request a SuperGold Card as evidence that the cardholder is eligible for a community services card:
a person described in regulation 290(1)﻿(ea):
a person described in regulation 290(2).

A person (C) required or requested to produce C’s SuperGold Card by a person entitled to require or request its production may be treated as if C does not hold a SuperGold Card (or, as the case requires, is not eligible for a community services card) if C does not produce it within a reasonable time.
If a SuperGold Card bears a code indicating that the cardholder is entitled to a community services card, it is not a breach of subclause (1) or (2) for the cardholder—

to lend it temporarily to a dependent child of the cardholder, or any other person who is for the time being looking after that dependent child, for the purpose only of obtaining for that dependent child services funded under the New Zealand Public Health and Disability Act 2000; or

to lend it temporarily to any other person for the purpose only of obtaining for the cardholder, or a dependent child of the cardholder, services funded under the New Zealand Public Health and Disability Act 2000.
